EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold air mass from Canada settled over western Montana and brought record cold temperatures, hard frost with crop damage and snowfall along the Continental Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several farms and orchards in the Missoula Valley saw damage to corn, tomatoes, squash, pumpkin and basil. A winery saw 25% loss to their grapes despite attempts at mitigation via watering and helicopter operations. The official low temperature at the Missoula International Airport was 25 degrees which broke the old daily record of 28 set back in 1988. It was also the 3rd coldest low in the first 15 days of September since records began in 1893.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very warm temperatures and light winds were seen as monsoonal moisture made its way north along the Rockies. Isolated to scattered showers and thunderstorms were seen.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very small and brief tornado was reported to have touched down 18 mi E of Fort Belknap.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system over the Pacific Northwest pushed moisture and instability into the region bringing scattered showers to the area. Stronger upper level energy moving through North Central MT during the afternoon/evening brought the area increased potential for severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail, measuring 1.75 inches (golf ball sized), was reported along with heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold air mass from Canada settled over western Montana and brought record cold temperatures, hard frost with crop damage and snowfall along the Continental Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter located in Ronan reported a tree with a three foot diameter base damaged by strong winds. Winds between 35 to 45 mph were reported from the Flathead to Mission Valleys as the cooler and more dense modified arctic air mass pushed southward. Flathead Lake saw choppy waves from the strong north to east winds causing the water to slop over into parking lots in Polson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold air mass from Canada settled over western Montana and brought record cold temperatures, hard frost with crop damage and snowfall along the Continental Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The arctic cold front pushed through during the evening of the 10th and brought several hours of light to moderate snow to MacDonald Pass and subsequently snow-covered roads. This front continued its way south affecting Homestake Pass on I-90 and also the Butte area. A trained spotter located 6 miles south-southwest of Butte at 5900 feet measured 3 inches of snow through 744 am MDT on the 11th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain, measuring 0.70 inches in 20 minutes, was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm and associated arctic front brought widespread snowfall and travel impacts to much of western Montana. Hardest hit areas were forecast to be from roughly I-90 and points north and west. The Missoula and Bitterroot valleys and most locations across SW MT were not hit as hard as forecast until midday Sunday, 12/28, when a convergence band set up and dropped 4 to 7 inches in Missoula, with 4 inches measured at the Missoula National Weather Service Forecast Office for the day. The accident rate was very high in the Missoula area, with 8-12 slideoffs/hour being reported from 2pm until 5pm. Impacts for Missoula lessened after 5pm on 12/28, but Butte continued to see moderate snow throughout the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As snow began to fall over the Flathead Valley, reports of crashes and slide offs began to increase sharply around 5 pm on the 27th. By the next morning, a storm spotter in Colombia Falls reported 13.5 inches of snow during this event. Over the Mission Valley, storm spotters reported 13 inches of storm total snow in Polson and 9 inches in Ronan. Strong winds associated with the arctic cold front caused numerous power outages over the Flathead Valley. Sustained winds of 43 mph over Flathead Lake near Yellow Bay was recorded by mesonet sensors for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving upper level wave and its associated cold front brought damaging winds to portions of southwest Montana. Temperatures warmed into the 60's and 70's ahead of the cold front which enhanced a stronger exchange of wind. Winds gusted between 45 and 55 mph in area causing temporary power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power company reported that downed power lines closed Interstate 15 at mile-marker 123 which is at the Rocker Interchange. The Bert Mooney Airport south of Butte reported a peak wind gust to 45 mph at 4:03 pm MST with the cold front passage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind gusts, measuring 50KT (58 MPH), were reported in Big Sandy, MT.

Total rainfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 2 inches over North-Central Montana with only 0.25 to 0.75 inches across Southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain, measuring over 2.00 inches in 24 hours, was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak up-slope flow and a relatively moist and unstable atmosphere combined with an upper-level disturbance to generate a few severe thunderstorms.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power company reported a power outage in the southern Hamilton area most likely due to strong wind gusts. Valley observation stations reported wind gusts between 40 to 50 mph while a few higher elevation stations reported wind gusts to 58 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind gusts, measuring 56KT (65MPH), were reported 13 mi WSW of Saint Mary, MT at the Logan Pass Visitor's Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the area allowed for valley cold pools to develop and temperature inversions to strengthen. This aided the development of freezing fog that caused roadways and other surfaces to become icy across portions of western Montana. A fatality occurred near Bigfork because of both operator error and black ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The MT DOT reported that US-2 had fog and icy conditions from Columbia Falls to Marias Pass. A weather spotter reported a glaze of ice on US-2 between Hungry Horse and Bad Rock Canyon with freezing drizzle at 9:29 am MST on Jan. 19.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moving north across the Superior produced heavy rainfall and a subsequent debris flow that damage forest service roads near the old West Mullan burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Debris flows were reported in Club Gulch in Flat Creek, Pardee Creek and at Shaw Gulch with moderate road damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 7 inches across the area resulted in bad road conditions.

The stronger thunderstorms were capable of producing damaging hail and wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail, measuring .88 inches (nickel sized), was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Westerly flow aloft and down-slope winds developed over the Rocky Mountain Front and North-Central plains. The strongest winds were along the Rocky Mountain Front but an approaching shortwave trough, allowed strong winds to spread across Central Montana. Isolated heavy snow was also reported, mainly around areas of higher terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow measuring 7.0 inches in 12 hours reported 11 mi SSW of East Glacier Park, MT at the Pike Creek SNOTEL; elevation 5930ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of high winds affected much of the area as a strong westerly flow aloft remained over the area. Mid-level winds during this time was in the 70 to 90 knot range with surface gusts exceeding 80 mph along the Rocky Mountain Front and as far east as Cut Bank. Across the rest of the area winds gusted as high as 70 mph as the front pushed across the region. Moisture also moved into the area bringing snow to some areas of Central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds measuring 50KT (58MPH) were reported 17 mi SW of Simms, MT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system brought high winds across the entire Billings Forecast Area. High pressure across the Great Basin area combined with a surface low pressure across the Dakotas resulted in very tight pressure gradients across the eastern half of Montana, as well as the northern portions of Wyoming. Sustained winds around 40 mph with gusts over 60 mph were common from the late morning hours through the afternoon. In Billings, a tree briefly knocked out power in a neighborhood while a business sign on Broadwater fell on a van in a parking lot, smashing the windshield. In addition, a semi-truck blew over on Interstate 90 near Greycliff. This was also a record setting day of winds for Billings. The Billings Airport recorded its highest average daily wind speed ever recorded at 33 mph. This broke the previous record of 32 mph set on March 27, 1956.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pacific moisture accompanied a warmer layer of air which moved over the colder surface temperatures of northeast Montana and created favorable conditions for freezing rain to occur during the evening hours of the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A bus carrying the Minot State University wrestling team was travelling east on  Highway 2.  They slid off the road just after crossing the Milk River bridge 2 miles west of Dodson.  Three people sustained injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of troughs moving east across the Northern Rockies brought snow to much of the region. Greatest precipitation amounts extended from the Rocky Mountain Front |east across South-Central MT. Steady moderate snow developed across North-Central MT as cooler air spread south into the region from Canada. Higher elevations saw significant snow amounts while the valleys received lower amounts.

A very unstable northwest flow aloft combined with a strong upper level disturbance that moved over the area brought heavy snow to portions of the Eastern Foothills of the Beartooth/Absaroka Mountains. In addition, very strong winds combined with snow cover, resulted in blizzard conditions for the Judith Gap area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A ground blizzard was reported due to high winds and blowing snow. This resulted in U.S. Highway 191 from Harlowton to Eddies Corner being closed due to poor visibilities and road conditions.  Multiple vehicle crashes were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the 4th time during the 2013-2014 winter season, an arctic air mass briefly penetrates westward over the Continental Divide and brought extreme wind chills to -31 F to portions of southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The MT DOT road sensor located at milepost 8.1 on MT-141, reported extreme wind chill values between -20 and -31 deg. F. Wind speeds varied from 10 to 19 mph during the morning of Jan. 5. Other wind chills observed(all in degrees F): -25 Helmville, -25 Garrison Junction, -20 MacDonald Pass and -19 at Bert Mooney Airport ASOS. Along the wind chills a few locations including MacDonald Pass saw snow, blowing and drifting snow at times. Deer Lodge reported snow, blowing snow and wind gusts to 23 mph between midnight and 3 am MST on Jan. 5 as the arctic cold front passed through. The Elliston COOP measured 7 of snow with 0.31 of precipitation between Jan. 4-5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Westerly flow aloft and down-slope winds developed over the Rocky Mountain Front and North-Central plains. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system brought high winds across the entire Billings Forecast Area. High pressure across the Great Basin area combined with a surface low pressure across the Dakotas resulted in very tight pressure gradients across the eastern half of Montana, as well as the northern portions of Wyoming. Sustained winds around 40 mph with gusts over 60 mph were common from the late morning hours through the afternoon. In Billings, a tree briefly knocked out power in a neighborhood while a business sign on Broadwater fell on a van in a parking lot, smashing the windshield. In addition, a semi-truck blew over on Interstate 90 near Greycliff. This was also a record setting day of winds for Billings. The Billings Airport recorded its highest average daily wind speed ever recorded at 33 mph. This broke the previous record of 32 mph set on March 27, 1956.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 58 mph and 69 mph were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly down-slope winds along the Rocky Mountain Front remained high over a few days as a generally westerly flow aloft remained in place. Stronger and gustier winds were felt through through a few favored passes and canyons along the Rocky Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds measuring 71KT (82MPH) were reported 3 mi SW of Cut Bank, MT at the Cut Bank Airport ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of high winds affected much of the area as a strong westerly flow aloft remained over the area. Mid-level winds during this time was in the 70 to 90 knot range with surface gusts exceeding 80 mph along the Rocky Mountain Front and as far east as Cut Bank. Across the rest of the area winds gusted as high as 70 mph as the front pushed across the region. Moisture also moved into the area bringing snow to some areas of Central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds measuring 103KT (119MPH) were reported 13 mi WSW of Saint Mary, MT at the Logan Pass Visitor's Center in Glacier National Park.

Pre-frontal rain began to fall on cold surfaces in the Flathead Valley. With ground temperatures near freezing, emergency managers reported slick road conditions with multiple accidents due to weather. Timing of the freezing rain was inopportune, as many were beginning Thanksgiving week travel, leading to even higher-than-usual impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Libby, MT law enforcement reported roads as a solid sheet of ice overnight. Local businesses also reported icy roads throughout Lincoln county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong west flow ahead of an upper level low pressure system is moving across Central Montana. Winds aloft strengthened and produced high winds along the Rockies and adjacent plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple skier triggered avalanches in the Bridger Mountains of Southwest Montana. No injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that moved into the region from Central Canada brought widespread continuous snow to much of Central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow, measured at 8.0 inches in 12 hours, was reported 15 mi SW of Dupuyer, MT at the Dupuyer Creek SNOTEL; elevation 5750 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed mid-level low tracked from southwest Idaho on Nov. 1 to near Cut Bank by early morning on Nov. 2. While this happened, heavy snow developed along and to the northwest of this feature in the mountains with snow levels generally above 4,000 feet. A few areas saw snow down to 3,000 feet in the Mission and Flathead Valleys. This was the first significant widespread mountain snowfall for the season, with many hunters and back-country users still in the high terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hoodoo Basin SNOTEL at 6,050 ft received 8��� of snow and 0.80��� of snow water equivalent.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough originating from the Gulf of Alaska dove southeast across the Pacific Northwest during the day on Dec. 24.  The base of the trough tracked across northern CA 4 pm PST on Dec. 24 to west-central Utah by 5 am MST on Dec. 25. It had a very moist plume of moisture that moved ahead of it and the precipitable water(a measure of the amount of moisture in the atmospheric layer) values were 200% of normal, which most likely contributed to the heavy precipitation values across northwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As of 6:30 am on the 24th, MDT reported 12 to 14��� of snow between Coram and West Glacier. On top of the morning snowfall report, the Pike Creek SNOTEL located 697 feet above Marias Pass reported an additional 11��� after this time-frame. Pike Creek saw a total snowfall of 17���.  MDT later reported that there were times when they had a hard time keeping up with this snow event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass pushed southward in western Montana Sunday night into Monday(Nov. 10). There was a period of rain on Sunday before the arctic air mass moved through setting up the stage for a flash freeze and snow combination by Sunday night. After the arctic cold front passed south through the Missoula Valley around midnight and entered the Bitterroot Valley, a period of heavy snow developed from Missoula south to Lolo and east to Clinton. Most roads were either snow-covered, or ice and snow-covered, which hampered travel during the Monday morning commute. These impacts were especially severe given near-record high temperatures earlier in the week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter estimated strong arctic winds gusting to 30 mph near Essex, MT. Glacier Park International Airport saw a peak gust of 48 mph when the arctic air began to spill into the Flathead Valley, and this wind likely topped 50 mph in the Bad Rock canyon region. Police dispatch reported wind knocking down multiple trees in West Glacier, MT. An additional hazard was rain, which fell before the arctic cold front. The flash-freezing of this water on roads contributed to very slick road conditions, as reported by spotters.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Glacier Park International Airport saw a peak gust of 48 mph when the arctic air began to spill into the Flathead Valley. At the airport, sustained 32 mph winds with gusts to 40 mph were commonplace, and visibility down to 0.75 mi was reported at times. These winds caused multiple trees falling across roadways in Whitefish and Kalispell. Power outages and ���treacherous��� road conditions impacted many and were reported by weather spotters. ASOS reports of rain before the cold winds explained why a weather spotter in Columbia Falls tweeted that there was a solid sheet of ice beneath the snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged warm advection pattern with a deep moisture plume resulted in abundant precipitation across the Northern Rockies.  Snow levels quickly rose through the event over much of the region, but northwest Montana held enough cold air to keep snow levels down to the valley floors for much of the event.  The mountains near the continental Divide from near the Bob Marshall north through Glacier National Park received heavy snowfall. Five inches of heavy, wet snow fell in the Bitterroot Valley causing widespread power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 2500 people in the Bitterroot Valley were without power for over 6 hours after 5 inches of extremely wet snow fell in and around Darby. Hamilton spotters reported 3 to 4 inches. Snow ratios were near 5:1 for this event. A local fire chief near Darby said it was the worst he's seen it in over 50 years in regards to call volume and number of broken tree limbs.

An additional hazard was the layer of ice below the snow due to the preceding rainfall in some places. In the morning school buses were stuck in uptown Butte delaying their arrival. Tens of weather-related accidents occurred in the Silver Bow county area. An emergency manager said this was definitely an above normal impact event for the Silver Bow County.

Slick roads and whiteout conditions reported by county sheriffs. Icy roads caused a very slow commute for NWS employees and workers across Missoula.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist pacific system interacted with a strong arctic front causing heavy snow over northwest Montana and near-blizzard conditions to Bad Rock Canyon near Columbia Falls, MT. Winds gusts of 40 to 50+ mph caused numerous trees to fall over power lines and homes in Glacier National Park. The arctic front continued to push southward bringing gusty northerly winds to the Mission Valley. At 3 pm Nov 29th, the arctic air spilled over Evaro hill and brought snow/blowing snow to Missoula for several hours. Due to the warm surface temperatures during the day, the initial band of snow melted and then refroze, causing icy roads and numerous accidents around Missoula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were reports of whiteout conditions by numerous spotters. A St. Regis hotel estimated a storm total snowfall of 6 inches. In addition, multiple accidents occurred along I-90 due to the slick road surface.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very tight pressure gradients were situated across the western portions of the Billings Forecast Area. This resulted in high winds across the Livingston and the Beartooth Foothills areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 50 to 58 mph with a peak gust of 73 mph were recorded at the Livingston Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very tight pressure gradients were situated across the western portions of the Billings Forecast Area. This resulted in high winds across the Livingston and the Beartooth Foothills areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 50 mph with a peak gust of 79 mph were recorded at the Stillwater Mine.

In Glacier National Park, about 20 structures were hit by large falling trees including a vehicle and six homes. No injuries were reported as a result of the damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A reinforcing arctic boundary brought very cold wind chills, especially for early November. Winds chill warnings were issued along the Continental Divide and for wind prone areas. Even though the wind chills did not quite reach defined wind chill criteria, they were issued because it was early in the season for such bitter values.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds of 16 mph and temperatures in the mid single digits lead to prolonged wind chills near -12 F over 5 hours on the morning of the 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm and associated arctic front brought widespread snowfall and travel impacts to much of western Montana. Hardest hit areas were forecast to be from roughly I-90 and points north and west. The Missoula and Bitterroot valleys and most locations across SW MT were not hit as hard as forecast until midday Sunday, 12/28, when a convergence band set up and dropped 4 to 7 inches in Missoula, with 4 inches measured at the Missoula National Weather Service Forecast Office for the day. The accident rate was very high in the Missoula area, with 8-12 slideoffs/hour being reported from 2pm until 5pm. Impacts for Missoula lessened after 5pm on 12/28, but Butte continued to see moderate snow throughout the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lookout Pass ski resort reported 17 inches of snow over a 24 hour period. Out of the 17 inches, 14 inches of snow fell between 4 pm MST Saturday and 5 am MST Sunday. MDT required that towing vehicles use chains after 5 pm on the 27th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist pacific system interacted with a strong arctic front causing heavy snow over northwest Montana and near-blizzard conditions to Bad Rock Canyon near Columbia Falls, MT. Winds gusts of 40 to 50+ mph caused numerous trees to fall over power lines and homes in Glacier National Park. The arctic front continued to push southward bringing gusty northerly winds to the Mission Valley. At 3 pm Nov 29th, the arctic air spilled over Evaro hill and brought snow/blowing snow to Missoula for several hours. Due to the warm surface temperatures during the day, the initial band of snow melted and then refroze, causing icy roads and numerous accidents around Missoula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the powerful arctic front made its way through Bad Rock Canyon, it brought damaging winds and blizzard-like conditions to Columbia Falls, MT and Kalispell, MT. Trees falling on power lines left 9000 Flathead Electric Cooperative members without power on Saturday, Nov 29. The ASOS at Glacier Park International airport recorded wind gusts of 48 mph, with 40 mph gusts being common for 11 hours and wind chill values down to -20 degrees F. Snowfall totals were estimated at 7 inches for Columbia Falls and 5 inches for the Kalispell airport. Roads conditions around the area reported blowing and drifting snow and ice.  Between early Saturday morning and Sunday afternoon, Montana Highway Patrol responded to more than 40 crashes in Flathead County alone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A reinforcing arctic boundary brought very cold wind chills, especially for early November. Winds chill warnings were issued along the Continental Divide and for wind prone areas. Even though the wind chills did not quite reach defined wind chill criteria, they were issued because it was early in the season for such bitter values.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The RAWS near Clearwater Junction reported wind chills as low as -17 F through the mornings of the 11th and 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Plentiful moisture from the southeast co-located with an area of surface low pressure and an unstable atmosphere allowed severe thunderstorms to develop and persist along a subsequent outflow boundary across eastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Border patrol at the Port of Scobey estimated a 60 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A group of seven snowmobilers was riding north of Cooke City when an 18 year old from Minnesota was caught in an avalanche on a steep, northwest facing slope on Crown Butte outside Cooke City. The victim was stopped on the slope while two others were riding on it when the avalanche was triggered. The others rode off, but the victim was caught and buried. Neither he, nor anyone in his party of seven, was wearing a beacon. He was fully buried and Cooke City Search and Rescue located him with a probe pole under six feet of debris near the toe of the avalanche about 1.5-2 hours later. The avalanche broke 700 feet wide and 2-5 feet deep on depth hoar crystals about 1 foot above the ground. The avalanche ran 800 feet vertical and 1600 feet slope distance. Debris ranged from 5 to 15 feet deep and covered an area 900 by 400 feet. Slope angles in the starting zone ranged from 36 to 38 degrees. For a more detailed report, please see the avalanche incidents report at http://www.mtavalanche.com.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A group of seven snowmobilers was riding north of Cooke City when an 18 year old from Minnesota was caught in an avalanche on a steep, northwest facing slope on Crown Butte outside Cooke City. Neither he, nor anyone in his party of seven, was wearing a beacon. The avalanche ran 800 feet vertical and 1600 feet slope distance. Debris ranged from 5 to 15 feet deep and covered an area 900 by 400 feet. Slope angles in the starting zone ranged from 36 to 38 degrees. For a more detailed report, please see the avalanche incidents report at http://www.mtavalanche.com.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching trough strengthened the pressure gradient on the east side of the Rocky Mountain Front and brought increased wind speeds to the region. Wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were seen along the Rocky Mountain Front, especially through favored canyons and mountain passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind gusts, measured at 53KT (61MPH), were reported 13 mi W of Bynum, MT at the Dellwo site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm and associated arctic front brought widespread snowfall and travel impacts to much of western Montana. Hardest hit areas were forecast to be from roughly I-90 and points north and west. The Missoula and Bitterroot valleys and most locations across SW MT were not hit as hard as forecast until midday Sunday, 12/28, when a convergence band set up and dropped 4 to 7 inches in Missoula, with 4 inches measured at the Missoula National Weather Service Forecast Office for the day. The accident rate was very high in the Missoula area, with 8-12 slideoffs/hour being reported from 2pm until 5pm. Impacts for Missoula lessened after 5pm on 12/28, but Butte continued to see moderate snow throughout the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the arctic front continued to move through western Montana, a snow band developed near Missoula and eventually made its way over Interstate 90 between Frenchtown and Superior. Multiple accidents and slide offs were reported with whiteout conditions under the heavy snow band.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front invaded the Billings Forecast Area during the evening hours on Christmas Eve. At the same time, an upper low was situated over Idaho. Light rain began during the early evening hours but quickly changed to wet snow. Road temperatures were above freezing resulting in the snow melting on contact. However, temperatures dropped quickly and the wet and slushy roads quickly froze which resulted in very icy road conditions for the next one to two weeks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Area Snotels received around 12 to 20 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow zone of unstable air and a break in the clouds allowed brief daytime heating to generate a line of severe thunderstorms during the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter estimated 60 mph winds while driving near Hinsdale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed mid-level low tracked from southwest Idaho on Nov. 1 to near Cut Bank by early morning on Nov. 2. While this happened, heavy snow developed along and to the northwest of this feature in the mountains with snow levels generally above 4,000 feet. A few areas saw snow down to 3,000 feet in the Mission and Flathead Valleys. This was the first significant widespread mountain snowfall for the season, with many hunters and back-country users still in the high terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Moss Creek SNOTEL reported 12 of snow. Impact-wise, difficult travel was reported by hunters and back-country users.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mild and moist Pacific air moved over an arctic air mass located across western Montana and caused a myriad of precipitation types including freezing rain, sleet and snow. Below freezing temperatures continued throughout the event in the valleys which caused untreated surfaces to be very slick. A fatality occurred in the Flathead Valley as a result of slick roads and driving too fast for conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Missoula International Airport received 1.7 of snow, sleet and freezing rain during the event. It initially started as snow with temperatures at 23 degrees and then started to mix with sleet by 6 am MST on Dec 4. It then changed to light freezing drizzle and rain by the evening hours causing black ice on side roads. The Bitterroot Valley saw an earlier change to freezing drizzle during the early morning hours. An observer in Florence reported 0.8 of snow before it changed to freezing drizzle. The slick conditions caused several vehicle slide-offs and accidents in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mild and moist Pacific air moved over an arctic air mass located across western Montana and caused a myriad of precipitation types including freezing rain, sleet and snow. Below freezing temperatures continued throughout the event in the valleys which caused untreated surfaces to be very slick. A fatality occurred in the Flathead Valley as a result of slick roads and driving too fast for conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Glacier Park International Airport observers reported a storm snowfall total of 5.3 with 0.44 melted liquid and temperatures only rising from the mid-teens to the lower 20's during the event. The reports of freezing rain on top of snow were mainly found from the southern half of Flathead Lake and points south. Other reports include: Near 9 of new snow 5 miles north of Bigfork, 7 at Lake Blaine, 6 near Polebridge, 6 east of Polson towards the Mission Mountains, and 5 north of Whitefish. A 17 year old boy died as a result of a head-on crash between Bigfork and Somers on Montana Highway 82 on Dec. 4. The crash happened on a curve where road conditions were particularly slick and the Montana Highway Patrol commented that the boy was driving too fast for the conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While a surface low tracked northeast into British Columbia during the morning of February 14th, moist and relatively milder air streamed over Idaho and western Montana. The warm front brought brief periods of intense snowfall across much of the area causing low visibilities for a brief time and slick roadways. After the warm front moved through, the air-mass became unstable with strong westerly winds aloft. This helped to form fairly organized graupel, snow and rain bands, some of which brought moderate impacts to area mountain passes. A ridge of high pressure moved in at night causing temperatures to cool and black ice to form.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of moderate snow fell at both Lolo and Lost Trail Passes especially associated with the warm front precipitation during the morning of Feb. 14th. Snow and rain bands had developed by the afternoon which brought brief periods of limited visibility. A rock slide occurred on Skalkaho highway 38 at mile-marker 7 around 5:53 pm MST blocking both lanes. It was subsequently cleared by 7 pm MST. The Lost Trail ski resort reported 8 of new snow 24 hours ending at 5:58 am MST Feb. 15. The Lolo Pass SNOTEL reported 4 of total snowfall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Warm springs SNOTEL reported 5 of snow with frontal passage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist pacific system interacted with a strong arctic front causing heavy snow over northwest Montana and near-blizzard conditions to Bad Rock Canyon near Columbia Falls, MT. Winds gusts of 40 to 50+ mph caused numerous trees to fall over power lines and homes in Glacier National Park. The arctic front continued to push southward bringing gusty northerly winds to the Mission Valley. At 3 pm Nov 29th, the arctic air spilled over Evaro hill and brought snow/blowing snow to Missoula for several hours. Due to the warm surface temperatures during the day, the initial band of snow melted and then refroze, causing icy roads and numerous accidents around Missoula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An arctic front spilled over Evaro Hill which brought snow to Missoula and Bitterroot Valleys. Due to warm road temperatures, snow initially melted then refroze. Icy road conditions led to over 30 slideoff incidents being reported by the DOT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pre-frontal rain began to fall on cold surfaces in the Flathead Valley. With ground temperatures near freezing, emergency managers reported slick road conditions with multiple accidents due to weather. Timing of the freezing rain was inopportune, as many were beginning Thanksgiving week travel, leading to even higher-than-usual impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported 10 crashes due to ice with at least 1 rollover. Over half of these accidents had injuries associated with them. Multiple trained spotters reported slippery roads with a couple reporting the roads as ice rinks.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in the Kalispell area by 9:45 am MST on Feb. 14 and ended a little after 1 pm MST. The Glacier Park International Airport reported a period of heavy snow with 1 falling in a ten minute period ending 10:23 am MST. This sudden change brought very low visibilities and snow-covered roads to much of the Flathead Valley. The Flathead County 911 dispatch reported 6 motor vehicle accidents between 10 and 11 am MST with a few of those being slide-offs. MTDOT reported snow-covered roads through the afternoon hours from Yellow Bay to Creston on Highway 35 and on Highway 206 north to Columbia Falls. Snowfall amounts include: 1.4 at Glacier Park Int. Airport, 3 Columbia Falls and 1 near Blue Bay on the east side of Flathead Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong jet stream moved across the Billings Forecast Area. Strong winds associated with this jet streak mixed down to the surface resulting in high winds across portions of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 58 mph were reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very slow moving trough of low pressure brought 8 to 10 inches of wet heavy snow to Homestake Pass. Snow became heavy over Homestake Pass around noon and was closed due to 12 semis stuck on the pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow became heavy over Homestake Pass around noon and was closed due to 12 semi-trailer trucks stuck on the pass. DOT officials reported 8 to 10 inches of wet heavy snow throughout this event and at one point they had 4 snow plows out at Homestake Pass. There were approximately 14 crashes/slideoffs from around 9 am to 10 pm within the area.  This includes two jack-knifed semis, both on Homestake Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough originating from the Gulf of Alaska dove southeast across the Pacific Northwest during the day on Dec. 24.  The base of the trough tracked across northern CA 4 pm PST on Dec. 24 to west-central Utah by 5 am MST on Dec. 25. It had a very moist plume of moisture that moved ahead of it and the precipitable water(a measure of the amount of moisture in the atmospheric layer) values were 200% of normal, which most likely contributed to the heavy precipitation values across northwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of moderate to heavy snow fell mainly during the early morning hours affecting Lookout Pass. Around 11 inches of snow fell which caused snow-covered roads and slick conditions. No crashes or slide-offs were reported during this period by the MT Department of Justice.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A moist and strong warm front brought a relatively brief period of heavy snow to Lookout Pass on I-90 from 9 am to 12 pm on Feb. 14. Two separate jack-knifed trucks were reported due to slick road conditions Friday evening, one at mile-marker 3 at 7:52 pm MST and another at mile-marker 24.5 at 4:54 pm MST.  By nightfall the skies cleared out and temperatures fell into the 20's helping to develop black ice. Another tractor trailer jack-knifed on the west bound lane at mile-marker 58 or 4.3 miles  north-northwest of Tarkio on I-90 at 1:15 am MST on Feb. 15. This incident blocked blocked both lanes until 2:36 am MST and was finally cleared by 3:22 am MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and blowing snow causes hazardous travel across western Montana. Around a foot of snow falls along the I-90 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP located at Lindbergh Lake or 8 miles south-southwest of Condon reported a storm total snowfall of 21.2 with 0.79 of precipitation, which happens to be the highest snow total reported across western Montana during this event. Other snowfall reports include: 20 Seeley Lake, 14.2 Greenough 2 NNE, and 10 in Potomac. Montana DOT reported periods of snowfall, poor visibility, blowing and drifting snow along MT-200 Greenough Hill to Helmville and also I-90 east of Missoula near Bonner. The Potomac schools had a 2 hour delay on Feb. 24. At 5:42 pm MST on Feb. 23, a weather spotter in Seeley Lake reported 2.5 per hour snowfall rates. At 3 am MST on Feb. 24, a car slid off the road and caused a mini-avalanche east of Bonner at mile-marker 3 on Highway 200 partially burying the vehicle and roadway. Traffic was backed up for 2 hours while crews and several plows worked to clear the snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and blowing snow causes hazardous travel across western Montana. Around a foot of snow falls along the I-90 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP located 3 miles west of Deer Lodge reported a storm total snowfall of 17 with 0.49 of precipitation. The atmospheric conditions of being just to the north of the arctic frontal boundary that brought an intensification of snow near the Deer Lodge area may have been similar to what had happened near the Florence & Stevensville areas. Other snowfall amounts include: 8.2 Anaconda 7.4 NW and 4.6 Philipsburg. Montana DOT reported snow, blowing and drifting snow from MacDonald Pass west to portions of I-90 near Garrison Junction to Drummond. Numerous automobile accidents and slide-offs were reported especially between 2 and 6 pm MST on Feb. 23. Chains were required for towing vehicles on MacDonald Pass for a period of time between Feb. 23-24.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong upper level winds developed across the northern Rockies ahead of a couple low pressure troughs entering the region. The first crossed the Pacific Northwest with strong mid-level winds across the Rockies and adjacent plains. At lower levels, the cold front came across the Canadian border Sunday bringing snow to much of Central Montana. The second trough crossed the Canadian border Monday along with a second push of moisture and very cold air.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind gusts, measured at 52KT (60MPH), were reported at the Heart Butte RAWS.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports across this zone were highly varied due to the focused and banded nature of this snow storm.  Reports of 5 inches or less were common near Wibaux and points north while at least 12 inches of snowfall were reported multiple times across southern portions of the zone.  A maximum of 13 inches was reported by the cooperative observer at 9 NW Carlyle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several reinforcing bouts of arctic air bring snow and extreme cold to western Montana. By the morning of Feb. 6, many valley locations saw air temperature readings between 30 and 40 degree below zero which caused businesses and school systems to close.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bert Mooney Airport ASOS located south of Butte reported wind chills below -20 F from early morning on Feb. 4 through the morning of Feb. 7 with the coldest wind chill value of -50 deg. F observed around 6:53 am MST on Feb. 6. The MT DOT sensor located 8 miles north of Avon on Highway 141, measured a wind chill of -59 deg. F from 7:45 am through 8:16 am MST on Feb. 6. Also a wind chill of -57 F was observed in Elliston via the mesonet. The Discovery ski area closed due to the extreme cold between Feb. 5-6. The coldest minimum temperatures recorded during this period were: -37 F 3 miles west of Deer Lodge, -36 F Philipsburg and Bert Mooney Airport, -34 F Anaconda and Elliston.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gallatin County Sheriff���s Office received a report of an unknown number of skiers that had been caught up in an avalanche north of Bridger Bowl. ||Rescuers found one injured skier. The skier was a 32 year old male from Bozeman that had been skiing out of bounds north of Bridger Bowl in what is called the Barney Avalanche Slide Path. The skier caused the avalanche at the ridge breaking a large cornice free and landed approximately 450 yards down the hill. The skier was able to dig his way out of the snow. Rescuers moved the skier to the Bridger Bowl Ski Patrol building to provide further medical assistance. The skier was then transported to Bozeman Deaconess Hospital for his injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several reinforcing bouts of arctic air bring snow and extreme cold to western Montana. By the morning of Feb. 6, many valley locations saw air temperature readings between 30 and 40 degree below zero which caused businesses and school systems to close.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ronan Airport AWOS recorded a low temperature of -38 deg. F at 5:15 am MST on Feb. 6, and a power outage was reported in Ronan.  The Glacier Park International Airport located north of Kalispell saw extreme wind chills between Feb. 4-6 and saw its coldest wind chill reading of -30 at 5:55 am MST Feb. 6. The Polson Kerr Dam COOP recorded -22 on Feb. 6, which makes it the coldest all-time temperature for the month of February since records began in March 1951. Other low temperatures recorded include: -42 St. Ignatius 5 north, -28 St. Ignatius, -24 Whitefish, and -23 Creston. On Feb. 5, the Bigfork school districts closed due to dangerous wind chills. On Feb. 6, the St. Ignatius schools closed due to extreme cold and wind chills.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter located in Columbia Falls reported brief episodes of near white-outs, blowing snow with northeast winds gusting to 27 mph during the early morning hours of Feb. 3. The Polson COOP observed 3 of snow on Feb. 3, which according to the DOT fell mainly during the early morning hours. Easterly winds over 40 mph were observed by the Flathead Lake Biological Station Yellow Bay Point sensor on the morning of Feb. 3. Other snowfall amounts include: 4 Ronan and 3 Arlee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several reinforcing bouts of arctic air bring snow and extreme cold to western Montana. By the morning of Feb. 6, many valley locations saw air temperature readings between 30 and 40 degree below zero which caused businesses and school systems to close.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Missoula International Airport ASOS reported wind chills less than -20 deg. F during this period.  The coldest wind chill reading recorded was -37 deg. F at 8:53 am MST on Feb. 6 when the temperature was -23 and wind was 4.6 mph. On the morning of Feb. 6, a power outage occurred on the east side of Florence where minimum temperatures had reached -32 deg. F. The Stevensville school district closed due to power outages caused by the extreme cold. The Stevensville COOP recorded a low temperature of -21 on Feb. 6.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS employee reported that snow was falling at least 1 per hour rates during this time period from Haugan to Lookout Pass. The MT DOT reported that several semi-trucks had spun out and jack-knifed due to the snowy conditions. Interstate 90 was closed for a period of time around 3:34 pm MST. The NWS employee reported again at 4:20 pm MST that probably small cars had no chance to travel near the Lookout Pass area due to the deep snow on the sides.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist upper level wave brought a period of heavy snow early on Feb. 20 and as it moved through the region, very unstable conditions helped to form heavy snow bands. These snow bands lasted through Friday Feb. 21.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow bands brought heavy snowfall rates between 1 to 2 per hour across portions of Sanders and Mineral Counties. Montana DOT reported heavy snow along I-90 and MT-200 at times throughout the morning hours. Mineral County dispatch reported periodic power outages due to the heavy snow in Superior and also in St. Regis. The St. Regis schools were closed due to the heavy snow and slick roads. Heavy snow brought whiteout conditions from Tarkio to Frenchtown on I-90 around 11 am MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist upper level wave brought a period of heavy snow early on Feb. 20 and as it moved through the region, very unstable conditions helped to form heavy snow bands. These snow bands lasted through Friday Feb. 21.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Missoula International Airport received 5 inches of snow in 5 hours from a heavy snow band that was over the Missoula Valley. A weather spotter located one mile north of Florence reported 7 of snow in 8 hours at 5:36 am MST. A weather spotter located 2 miles west of Charlos Heights estimated that 10 of snow fell in 12 hours ending at 6:36 am MST. Multiple motor vehicle slide-offs and accidents were reported from Missoula south to Hamilton due to the snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass combined with effective winds mixing down from the mid-layers of the atmosphere to produced dangerously cold wind chills across portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While many locations in this zone recorded only marginal and sporadic wind chills of -40 or worse, they were more prevalent and steady at the McDonalds MT-13 DOT site, beginning at 8:25 PM on February 28th and ending at 11:05 AM on March 1st.  The coldest wind chill of -51 occurred between 5:40 AM and 5:55 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass combined with effective winds mixing down from the mid-layers of the atmosphere to produced dangerously cold wind chills across portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although wind chills of -40 or worse began at 11:05 PM on February 28th, they did not last for an hour or more until just after midnight on the morning of March 1st. Wind chills improved to warmer than -40 at 10:25 AM on March 1st.  The coldest wind chill of -50 occurred between 3:50 AM and 6:50 AM on March 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and blowing snow causes hazardous travel across western Montana. Around a foot of snow falls along the I-90 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An avalanche buried two snowmobilers in the West Cabinet Range, about 17 miles southwest of Troy or about two miles north of Spark Peak. One person survived after being partially buried while the other person, a 49 year old male, died after being buried under 4 to 6 feet of compacted snow. The avalanche was classified as a D3 sized avalanche which means that it was a soft slab remotely triggered by a snowmobile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and blowing snow causes hazardous travel across western Montana. Around a foot of snow falls along the I-90 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP located one mile west of Haugan measured a storm total snowfall of 14 with 0.84 of precipitation. Other snowfall reports include: 11.4 Evaro .7 miles north-northwest, 10 Thompson Falls 9.3 miles northwest, 7.5 Alberton, 7 Thompson Falls and 5.2 Heron 2 miles northwest.  Fifteen motor vehicle slide-offs were reported mainly occurring on I-90 between Superior and Alberton. A motor vehicle accident near mile-marker 82 on I-90 west at 12:48 pm MST on Feb. 23, resulted in an injury after a semi-truck lost control at the Nine Mile bridge. On Feb. 24 because of the weather, the Superior and St. Regis school systems closed while the Alberton schools had a 2 hour delay. Montana DOT reported blowing and drifting snow causing limited visibility at Evaro Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and blowing snow causes hazardous travel across western Montana. Around a foot of snow falls along the I-90 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of light snow fell on Feb. 22 causing US-93 to become slick. Three persons died, while two were injured in an automobile accident around 5:30 pm MST near Trader Brothers on US-93 south. According to the Montana Highway Patrol both speed and the road conditions were a factor in the crash.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lolo Hot Springs COOP reported a storm total of 10 with 0.57 of precipitation. A weather spotter located 7 miles south-southeast of Hamilton reported a storm total snowfall of 11.5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A generally unsettled pattern persisted through this period. Lingering low level moisture, daytime heating, and weak disturbances moving through the moist westerly flow aloft, brought scattered showers with isolated thunderstorms to portions of South-Central and Southwest Montana. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration winter storm impacted most of North-Central and Southwest Montana with widespread snow and blowing snow, near record cold temperatures and |frigid wind chills. Numerous Winter Storm Warnings and Winter Weather Advisories were in effect through the event. Pacific moisture increased across Montana while a Canadian cold front from Saskatchewan/Alberta moved into the region. Precipitation continued to increase with the cold frontal passage as moist, westerly flow aloft overran arctic surface air. This pattern persisted through the weekend, bringing widespread accumulating snowfall to Central and Southwest Montana. In the system's wake, bitterly cold temperatures and wind chills moved in to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow measuring 6.0 inches in 12 hours was reported 14 mi SSE of Ulm, MT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration winter storm impacted most of North-Central and Southwest Montana with widespread snow and blowing snow, near record cold temperatures and |frigid wind chills. Numerous Winter Storm Warnings and Winter Weather Advisories were in effect through the event. Pacific moisture increased across Montana while a Canadian cold front from Saskatchewan/Alberta moved into the region. Precipitation continued to increase with the cold frontal passage as moist, westerly flow aloft overran arctic surface air. This pattern persisted through the weekend, bringing widespread accumulating snowfall to Central and Southwest Montana. In the system's wake, bitterly cold temperatures and wind chills moved in to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill of -41 degrees Fahrenheit reported 7 mi S of Neihart, MT at the Porphyry Peak RAWS.

An isolated severe thunderstorm produced 1 inch diameter hail in Stillwater County.
A severe thunderstorm produced a microburst across the eastern portions of Carter County. Considerable damage occurred in the Ekalaka area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Considerable damage occurred in the town of Ekalaka. The door from a newspaper building was blown off its hinges. An 80 foot tall 36 inch diameter Cottonwood tree was blown over along with numerous trailers being overturned.  There was tree damage and power lines down all over town resulting in power outages.  Other damages included blown down distribution poles and broken windows. In addition, four hangers and an enclosed private aircraft at the county airport suffered severe damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid/upper level trough and associated 40-50kt wind max moved across an increasingly unstable airmass across eastern Montana. Daytime heating and large scale ascent combined with a low-level east/southeast flow helped support storm updraft rotation which produced a few severe thunderstorms across the area.

A severe thunderstorm packing high winds and very heavy rain hit the Baker area during the early evening hours of the 16th.  As a result, many tree branches were down around town. The hardest hit area was the Fallon County Fair in Baker where singer Rick Springfield was scheduled to play shortly after the storm hit.  Some minor damage and injuries were reported at the fair.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sheriff Dispatch reported that an 18 inch diameter tree was uprooted, along with many tree branches down in Baker from the very strong winds. In addition, tin roofing material was ripped from the firehouse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm produced a severe wind gust about 3 miles northeast of Mill Iron in Carter County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A steel wheelbarrow was flipped over and some tree branches 3-4 inches in diameter were broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Diffluent southwest flow ahead of a trough brought instability and thunderstorms to Central Montana during the afternoon. East surface winds brought higher dew points to the area. The main threats from these storms were gusty winds and small hail. Convective activity decreased after midnight as the trough axis moved east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of .62 inches reported.

The snowpack during most of the month of May was around 150% of average. There was significant melting of the snowpack below 8000 feet during the month. The melting snowpack resulted in rising river levels and river flooding. The main impacts of the high river flows were fast flows, low-land flooding and bank erosion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clarks Fork of the Yellowstone crested during the early evening on the 27th when the river was out of its banks. The river took out a tree and power pole and line which landed on the historical USGS measurement site for the river gauge at Edgar. The historical measurement site was smashed, but the electronic gauge was unharmed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The snowpack during most of the month of May was around 150% of average. There was significant melting of the snowpack below 8000 feet during the month. The melting snowpack resulted in rising river levels and river flooding. The main impacts of the high river flows were fast flows, low-land flooding and bank erosion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some low-land flooding occurred along the Clarks Fork of the Yellowstone in Laurel. Yellowstone County Department of Emergency Services reported that Byam Road in Laurel was closed during the morning of the 27th as water greater than 6 inches in depth was flowing over the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough of low pressure, a cold front, and plentiful surface moisture combined with a moderate to very unstable atmosphere to produce and sustain many severe thunderstorms across most of eastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local newspaper reported two power poles were knocked down.  Power failure continued for two hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough of low pressure, a cold front, and plentiful surface moisture combined with a moderate to very unstable atmosphere to produce and sustain many severe thunderstorms across most of eastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated to 80 mph and blew shingles off a roof, and broke a window. Duration of very strong winds was at least 20 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a microburst across the eastern portions of Carter County. Considerable damage occurred in the Ekalaka area.

An upper level wave initiated strong thunderstorms across eastern Washington into Northern Idaho during the afternoon of August 2. The majority of these storms brought damaging wind that extended into northwest Montana. Above normal temperatures with dry surface conditions contributed to strong outflow winds from Heron to Troy to Libby. As a side note the COOP observer located 18 miles north of Troy reported a high of 99 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lincoln County Emergency Manager reported that thunderstorm winds downed numerous trees and power lines in the Troy area.

As a side note the COOP observer located 18 miles north of Troy reported a high of 99 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager from Lincoln County reported that numerous trees and power lines were down with roads blocked in the Libby area. The Zonolite RAWS located at 4,200 feet MSL reported a 51 mph wind gust at the 21:06 MST observation time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong upper level low that originated from the Gulf of Alaska, moved across northwest Washington on the evening of July 23 into south-central Alberta Canada by the evening of July 24. A mid-level wave tracked north-northeast across eastern Washington State on the afternoon of July 23 helping to initiate several severe thunderstorms. These storms brought wind damage that extended into northwest Montana. A few discrete strong thunderstorms developing over southwest Montana producing large hail near Butte.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported multiple power poles down, along with power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A single lightning bolt killed cattle at a ranch located in Darby, Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A single lightning bolt struck small crab-apple trees at a ranch located on Tin Cup Road, killing all 45 cattle crowded together. The Black Angus cattle consisted of cows, calves and a prize bull that died. A conservative damage estimate calculated by the NWS Missoula office is around $100,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough of low pressure, a cold front, and plentiful surface moisture combined with a moderate to very unstable atmosphere to produce and sustain many severe thunderstorms across most of eastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Glasgow airport ASOS measured a wind gust of 59 mph.  Winds continued gusting above 58 mph for nearly 1 hour.  These winds were a combination of thunderstorm wind gusts and post-frontal wind.  The peak wind gust was 60 mph.  Numerous large tree branches were down and some roof shingles were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough of low pressure, a cold front, and plentiful surface moisture combined with a moderate to very unstable atmosphere to produce and sustain many severe thunderstorms across most of eastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet site at Saint Marie measured a peak wind of 67 mph. These winds were a combination of thunderstorm wind gusts and post-cold frontal winds.  The strong and severe winds continued for an hour and 5 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm packing high winds and very heavy rain hit the Baker area during the early evening hours of the 16th.  As a result, many tree branches were down around town. The hardest hit area was the Fallon County Fair in Baker where singer Rick Springfield was scheduled to play shortly after the storm hit.  Some minor damage and injuries were reported at the fair.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A metal roof was blown off a shed and into the corrals resulting in damage to the corrals. A door was blown off a shed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold arctic air mass from the Canadian prairie pushed into the region and interacted with some lingering Pacific moisture to generate the first widespread accumulating snow of the season.  Totals ranged from 4 to 6 inches for the most part across the affected area.  Totals were up to 9 inches in the Little Rocky Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wide range of snow amounts fell across the large expanse of Garfield County. Reports of 2 to 4 inches were common across eastern and southern portions of the county, while reports of 5 to 6 inches were more common over the higher terrain of western and northwestern Garfield County, with a maximum of 8 inches of snow reported by a trained spotter located 17 WNW of Jordan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level wave initiated strong thunderstorms across eastern Washington into Northern Idaho during the afternoon of August 2. The majority of these storms brought damaging wind that extended into northwest Montana. Above normal temperatures with dry surface conditions contributed to strong outflow winds from Heron to Troy to Libby. As a side note the COOP observer located 18 miles north of Troy reported a high of 99 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated thunderstorm winds between 50 to 60 mph that caused tree damage and power outages in the area. The wind developed 10 to 12 minutes before brief heavy rain fell. They reported that the wind caused 4 big healthy fir and one yellow pine tree with a diameter of up to 2 feet to fall over.  They also observed a few cottonwood trees with their tops blown off. A 200 person awning from a wedding was damaged while a part of the roof trim from the Bull Lake Fire Department blew off as a result of the wind. Timing is based on reports and post-radar analysis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level wave initiated strong thunderstorms across eastern Washington into Northern Idaho during the afternoon of August 2. The majority of these storms brought damaging wind that extended into northwest Montana. Above normal temperatures with dry surface conditions contributed to strong outflow winds from Heron to Troy to Libby. As a side note the COOP observer located 18 miles north of Troy reported a high of 99 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP observer located 2.4 miles northwest of Heron reported strong gusty winds that caused the top 30 feet of a 70 year old sub-alpine fir to topple over a power line near their house. They also reported that approximately 5 trees located within a quarter of a mile of their home were damaged by the wind. Most of those trees broke off about 30 to 40 feet up and fell in a eastward direction. Their community was without power for about 23 hours after the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread and at times moderate to heavy rainfall affected North-Central Montana as a broad, potent low pressure system with plenty of moisture and energy moved east over the Northern Rockies. Periods of rain and isolated thunderstorms also affected the region. Total rainfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 2 inches over North-Central Montana with only 0.25 to 0.75 inches across Southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain, measuring 2.23 inches in 24 hours, was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very wet and slow-moving low pressure system stalled over Montana and directed near-continuous rainfall over central and northeastern portions of the state for several days.  Rainfall totals ranged from 5 to 10 inches in the affected area.||Valley County damages reported by DES to FEMA were in excess of $1.12 million dollars including the cost of repairs and labor.  There were 36 locations across Valley County that were damaged (road, bridge and culverts).||Petroleum County damages reported by DES to FEMA were at $515,000 with 30 locations around the county having damage (road, bridge and culverts).||On October 9th, 2014 a presidential disaster declaration was granted for this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Beaver Creek near Hinsdale (BCHM8) rose above its flood stage of 14 feet at 3:01 PM MST on August 24th, crested in major flooding at 19.52 feet at 12:30 AM MST on August 26th, then fell back down below flood stage at 6:37 AM MST on August 29th.  The crest of 19.52 feet became the new record historical crest for that gauge.

There were 36 locations across Valley County that were damaged (road, bridge and culverts).||Petroleum County damages reported by DES to FEMA were at $515,000 with 30 locations around the county having damage (road, bridge and culverts).||On October 9th, 2014 a presidential disaster declaration was granted for this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 5 to 10 inches of rainfall fell in a four day period.  This caused massive flooding in area creeks and streams.  The most significant damage was from Flatwillow Creek into the Musselshell River.  Cooperative weather observers noted it was the highest they had ever seen the water on Flatwillow Creek, including the 2011 flood season.  It was high enough that the USGS gage on Flatwillow Creek was washed out and is gone, and the Flatwillow Road bridge was also washed out.  The County DES coordinator reported over 30 locations with damages to culverts, roads and bridges with damages of at least $515,000.

There were 36 locations across Valley County that were damaged (road, bridge and culverts).||Petroleum County damages reported by DES to FEMA were at $515,000 with 30 locations around the county having damage (road, bridge and culverts).||On October 9th, 2014 a presidential disaster declaration was granted for this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall in the basin with amounts ranging from 4 to 10 inches over a four day period caused significant flooding in all the streams in the basin, and eventually the rivers.  Willow Creek, Antelope Creek, Cherry Creek, Larb Creek, Brazil Creek all flooded during this time frame, blocking off many gravel roads around the county.  Valley County had at least 36 locations with damages and DES reported to FEMA the totals were $1.2 million.  Willow Creek Road from Fort Peck to the Pines had a very large area completely wiped out with a large culvert gone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rainfall from preceding days along with embedded thunderstorms led to flash flooding in the Cherry Creek Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The initial report of flash flooding came from an NWS employee who reported that Cherry Creek on the northwest side of Glasgow has risen out of its banks and is flooding with several inches of water over Skylark Road at Martin Coulee.  The Creek was actually flooding in the area about 4 miles north of Glasgow westward and then southward to the Milk River.  Many homeowners evacuated and had to move livestock and trailers out of the flooded waters.  This is the second highest the river has been in at least 4 decades (2011 was higher).   There were over a dozen homes with water in the basements and crawl spaces, and the water was so high, many people were unable to leave their homes because the driveways were flooded over with swift moving water.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Continuing with flooding through the end of August, the Milk River at Tampico (TMPM8) rose above flood stage and crested in August, then fell back down below flood stage at 12:30 AM MST on September 1st.

The increased pressure gradient over the region resulted in strong winds, with Northern Rocky Mountain Front locations seeing high wind criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind gusts, measuring 58KT (67MPH), were reported 8 mi SE of East Glacier Park, MT at the Deep Creek RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread and at times moderate to heavy rainfall affected North-Central Montana as a broad, potent low pressure system with plenty of moisture and energy moved east over the Northern Rockies. Periods of rain and isolated thunderstorms also affected the region. Total rainfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 2 inches over North-Central Montana with only 0.25 to 0.75 inches across Southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain, measuring 7.50 inches storm total, was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person from the public west of Deer Lodge estimated that quarter size hail fell during this event. Also a trained spotter located 1 mile west of Deer Lodge measured pea size hail and reported that large amounts of it had fallen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low-pressure system combined with a moderately-unstable atmosphere to form a series of squall lines and severe thunderstorms which moved over far eastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage was found at a new home site with a modular home and a large metal pole barn located 2.4 miles east of the intersection of Highway 16 and County Road 496.  There were two other home sites west of this home, with no obvious damage.  The newer home had a few missing shingles, but the new pole barn had the south door blown in with the south wall blown out to the southeast. There were a few trees that were snapped or cracked and dropped about 2-3 feet off the ground, including older Russian olive trees.  The damage to the pole barn was estimated to have been caused by 70-75 mph winds.  The homeowners were home and said the wind came from the west-northwest and there was a lot of dirt and gravel kicked up and thrown at them prior to their seeking shelter in a safe room in the pole barn.  The garage door was blown in and the south wall was blown out.  The garage door blew through the side wall and landed on a trailer near a tree row.   All the insulation and debris from the side wall collapse were blown eastward.  ||Another third of a mile east is the town site of McCabe, where there are a few homes, sheds and outbuildings.  This area had a large older Russian olive tree uprooted as well as a two healthy spruce trees uprooted that were at least 25 feet tall.  There was a shed damaged as well, but the two garage doors on it were open directly to the microburst winds, and the wood at the concrete footing was over 30 years old.  All the debris was blown eastward.  ||There was a lot of minor damage as well through the townsite.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding from a prolonged period of moderate to heavy rain was compounded by some strong to severe thunderstorms across portions of Phillips and Valley Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported flooding across Highway 2 and other streets in the town of Saco with 2 to 3 inches of standing water. City flood drain valves had been closed due to ongoing long-fused flooding along the Milk River and nearby tributaries. City runoff could not flow out of town when needed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding from a prolonged period of moderate to heavy rain was compounded by some strong to severe thunderstorms across portions of Phillips and Valley Counties.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to a spotter 1 mile west of Deer Lodge 0.80 of rain fell in a time span of 20 minutes. This rain resulted in street flooding in the town of Deer Lodge with water lapping doors of many businesses. The Powell County fair and rodeo were impacted by the flooding especially the fairgrounds exhibition center. Prisoners from the state prison were summoned to help setup sandbags to help mitigate the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two upper level waves brought snow and freezing rain to portions of western Montana providing for slick roadways. One wave moved through between Jan. 7-8 and the second wave moved through between Jan. 8-9. Snow bands oriented from west-northwest to east-southeast developed by the morning of Jan. 10 and brought periods of heavy snow to western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer measured a total snowfall of 6.1 with 0.33 precipitation. Road surfaces from Bonner to Seeley Lake were slick at times through this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong upper level low that originated from the Gulf of Alaska, moved across northwest Washington on the evening of July 23 into south-central Alberta Canada by the evening of July 24. A mid-level wave tracked north-northeast across eastern Washington State on the afternoon of July 23 helping to initiate several severe thunderstorms. These storms brought wind damage that extended into northwest Montana. A few discrete strong thunderstorms developing over southwest Montana producing large hail near Butte.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several 6 inch diameter limbs blown down from a tall and very sturdy tree in the location of the inflow of a supercell thunderstorm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Missoula International Airport experienced freezing rain between 1:04 am and 3:46 am MST, and from 6 to 8 am MST on Jan. 9. There was a coating of glaze/ice observed on sidewalks and on side roads from Frenchtown south to Florence. On Jan. 10, intense snow bands brought up to 4 of snow to Stevensville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold air mass from Canada settled over western Montana and brought record cold temperatures, hard frost with crop damage and snowfall along the Continental Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches of snow fell across the region as an arctic air mass pushed in from east of the Divide. The Going-to-the-Sun Road was closed from Big Bend on the west side to Jackson Glacier Overlook on the east side of Glacier National Park due to wind, snow, ice and limited visibility. At Marias Pass on US-2, snow fell with visibility being reduced to 1/2 mile at times. A weather spotter located nine miles north-northeast of Polebridge reported 7 inches of snow at 841 am MDT on the 11th. The COOP station in Polebridge saw a low of 16 degrees on the morning of the 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient across the area resulted in strong wind gusts across portions of Wheatland County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 64 mph was recorded at the Wheatland County agrimet site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tight pressure gradients along the eastern Rocky Mountain Front Range resulted in a period of high winds across portions of the Billings Forecast Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of wind gusts to 63 mph were recorded at Tipi Village.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The seasons first blast of arctic air arrived on November 9th. An arctic front moved through the Billings Forecast Area during the afternoon and evening hours of the 9th. The front ushered in a northerly upslope flow which resulted in heavy snow across portions of the Billings Forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 inches was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few wind gusts to 59 mph were reported in Judith Gap.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 4 to 10 inches along with blizzard like conditions resulted in emergency travel only across portions of Carbon County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low over Idaho brought an upslope flow resulting in heavy snow to the Beartooth/Absaroka Mountains and adjacent foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Area Snotels reported 12 to 18 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist southwest flow ahead of a cold front brought a period of heavy snow to Lookout Pass while several valley locations saw several inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP located at 9 miles north-northeast of Yaak at 3,075 ft. MSL reported 10.5 of snow with 0.66 liquid equivalent. The Garver Creek SNOTEL located 11.3 miles north-northwest of Yaak in the Purcell Mountains at 4,250 ft. measured 9 of snow with 0.70 liquid.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and warm air mass brought freezing levels up to between 6,000 and 7,000 feet MSL on Feb. 27 and brought freezing rain to western Montana. Yet another arctic air mass pushed into western Montana and this time there was enough transportable snow to cause blizzard conditions in places. There were reports of severe drifting and severe driving conditions causing an unusually high amount of vehicle slide-offs and accidents. A rare urban avalanche took the life of a 68 year old woman and injured two others in a northern Missoula suburb.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person from the public in Hungry Horse reported whiteout conditions through Bad Rock Canyon due to blowing and drifting snow. Also extreme wind chills to -21 deg. F were reported from Essex to Marias Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just before midnight on January 11, precipitation developed in the higher elevations along the border of Idaho and Montana as milder and relatively moist air pushed into the region ahead of a strong Pacific storm system. An upper level trough with an above normal moisture content from subtropical origins combined with a strong upper level jet tracked through the Northern Rockies from January 11-12. This system brought heavy precipitation in the form of mountain snow & blowing snow, valley mixed precipitation including freezing rain due to below freezing ground temperatures across northwest Montana, high winds, wind loading of snow on eastern aspects in higher elevations elevating the avalanche risk and the risk for rock slides. Snow bands brought more snow to mountain passes and valley locations alike as the trough exited the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The MT DOT sensor located at milepost 47 on Highway 83 measured a gust to 51 mph at 1:59 pm. The Missoula 911 dispatch reported that there were trees down along Highway 200 and 83 due to the wind. The Snowbowl Ski Resort has to shut down two ski lifts due to the strong winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lost Trail Ski Resort measured a storm total snowfall of 13.5 in 13 hours and 34 minutes. The MT DOT required chains on towing vehicles at Lolo pass Saturday evening, Jan. 11 through the morning of Jan. 12. From 8 pm MST to 12 am MST Jan. 12, the Lolo Pass SNOTEL reported 4 of heavy snow. Lolo Pass received 14 inches of snow with 2.20 of snow water equivalent from Jan. 10-12.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person from the public located in Hall reported strong gusty winds near 60 mph. There were a few trees down, mainly dead Lodgepole Pine with diameters around 16 inches at the base. The MT DOT sensor located 8 miles north of Avon on Highway 141, recorded a peak wind gust to 59 mph while its highest sustained wind speed was 45 mph. Other wind reports include: 58 mph 3 miles NNW Racetrack and 53 mph 2 miles WSW of Deer Lodge. The combination of the recent rains and high winds helped to dislodge several rocks onto MT-1 at Flint Creek Hill by the late afternoon.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: When the cold front passed through Deer Lodge Airport around 3:20 pm MST, visibility dropped to one-quarter mile due to snow and blowing snow while winds gusted up to 53 mph. These similar conditions were reciprocated at MacDonald Pass by 4:05 pm and at Homestake Pass by 5:10 pm MST with the front. Snow and blowing snow was reported by a spotter in Anaconda from 4:45 pm to 6 pm MST, and they measured 3.5 of snow in 4 hours. Other snowfall amounts include: 7.5 Georgetown Lake, 6 Discovery Ski Resort and 1.5 7 miles south of Butte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a very active pattern, the mountains along the Idaho and Montana continued to receive heavy snowfall which brought impacts to travel, especially along I-90 and US-12. A cold front brought strong gusty winds that brought limited visibility and hazardous driving conditions at Marias Pass and MacDonald Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lookout Pass Ski resort reported 14 of heavy snow in 12 hours while the MT DOT required chains for towing vehicles from 7 pm Feb. 18 through 5:30 am MST on Feb. 19.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lolo Pass SNOTEL reported 7 of total snowfall with 0.60 of snow water equivalent.
