EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP located at the Eureka Ranger Station reported 1.1 of snow on Feb. 28. Light snow was reported across the area during this event keeping roads slick at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several reinforcing bouts of arctic air bring snow and extreme cold to western Montana. By the morning of Feb. 6, many valley locations saw air temperature readings between 30 and 40 degree below zero which caused businesses and school systems to close.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Seeley Lake Ranger Station COOP recorded low temperatures in the mid-20s below zero Fahrenheit for 4 days straight. The coldest low temperature recorded was -41 deg. F on the morning of Feb. 6. This COOP also observed its coldest maximum temperature of -1 on Feb. 6 which broke the all-time February coldest maximum temperature of 2 degrees set back on Feb. 8, 1994, since records began in Oct. of 1936. The Snowbowl ski area closed down due to a power outage and dangerous wind chills between Feb. 4-5. The Point 6 RAWS sensor at 7,905 ft., recorded a wind chill of -63 deg. F at 7:59 am MST on Feb. 5. Other coldest minimum temperatures include: -37 Potomac, -34 F Condon, -33 F Greenough Hill, -32 F Condon and -31 F 3 miles northeast of Bonner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of very warm temperatures ranging from 50 to 60 degrees occurred after an above normal snowfall winter across western Montana. This combined with moderate to heavy rainfall and high snow levels resulted in a quick melt of snowpack in various locations. Impacts felt included road closures due to avalanche danger, flooding of the Little Bitterroot River, rock slides across northwest Montana and lowland flooding across the Bitterroot Valley. President Obama declared a major disaster in the state of Montana which included Sanders, Lake and Ravalli Counties to help with the flood cleanup costs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The DES coordinator for Sanders County reported that the Little Bitterroot River flooded with 2 to 3 feet of water observed in places. The Little Bitterroot Road from Anderson's Corner to Sloan's Bridge was closed for 4 days. According to a river gauge located at one of the river's headwaters, Mill Creek, the discharge rate peaked at 238 cubic feet per second at 3:15 pm MST on March 6. In 31 years, this value surpassed the previous record of 53 cubic feet per second set back in 1986. A local rancher reported that this was the worst flooding he had ever seen. In terms of damage, the Little Bitterroot Road experienced minor damage with several culverts that needed to be replaced while homes were spared. Observations were sparse in the region, but a COOP observer located 2 miles northwest of Heron reported a 48 hour precipitation total of 1.99 between March 9-10. The combination of above normal snowfall that fell across western Montana in February, warm day and night-time temperatures and the rainfall contributed to substantial snow-melt in this event.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing was the first impact to cause black ice on I-90 between St. Regis and Frenchtown with a few accidents reported earlier on Feb. 27. The rain changed to moderate to heavy snow by nightfall. A NWS employee located one mile north of Alberton  reported 11 of snow at 5 am MST Feb. 28. Montana DOT reported blowing snow along sections of I-90 from Frenchtown to Alberton and also at Evaro Hill. Other reports include: 9 Evaro and 8 Haugan 1 west.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and warm air mass brought freezing levels up to between 6,000 and 7,000 feet MSL on Feb. 27 and brought freezing rain to western Montana. Yet another arctic air mass pushed into western Montana and this time there was enough transportable snow to cause blizzard conditions in places. There were reports of severe drifting and severe driving conditions causing an unusually high amount of vehicle slide-offs and accidents. A rare urban avalanche took the life of a 68 year old woman and injured two others in a northern Missoula suburb.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain fell from 4 to 9 pm MST on Feb. 27 along I-90 from Drummond to the Butte area and also along MT-1 from Anaconda to Hall. As the arctic air moved in combined with falling moderate to heavy snow, blowing and severe drifting developed. This contributed to the Montana DOT listing I-90 from Phosphate to Warm Springs, MT-1 Drummond to Hall as Severe Driving conditions from 12 am MST to 10:30 am MST on Feb. 28. It also reported severe driving conditions on I-90 from Gold Creek to Warm Springs from 8 to 11 am MST on March 1. Extreme wind chills to -29 F were reported at 8:24 pm MST 7 miles north of Helmville on Feb. 28. Thirty-eight slide-off and motor vehicle accidents were reported during this event. There were 11 reports of stranded vehicles as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very impressive arctic air mass set up over the region by the end of February. A very mild and moist system brought moderate to heavy snow to northwest Montana. Places like Eureka that typically experience a rain/snow shadow from the moist westerly events were able to receive more snow because the arctic air mass was so deep. The moisture content of the weather system, as measured by weather balloons upstream, came in at 181% of normal. This system helped to erode the cold air already in place in the valleys of west-central Montana, caused very slick roads due to freezing rain which led to school closures on March 3rd. This was one of the biggest snow events for the season  across northwest Montana and caused a few avalanches in a few places but did not threaten anyone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Montana DOT reported heavy snow overnight between Mar. 2-3 and estimated that 8 to 12 inches had fallen along Highway 2 south of Libby. They remarked that this was the heaviest snow event for the season. Montana DOT plow drivers in Troy estimated 12 fell there as well. Other snow amounts include: 12 Rexford 7 NNE, 8 Olney, 8.5 Yaak 9 NNE and 6.3 1 E Eureka.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a very active pattern, the mountains along the Idaho and Montana continued to receive heavy snowfall which brought impacts to travel, especially along I-90 and US-12. A cold front brought strong gusty winds that brought limited visibility and hazardous driving conditions at Marias Pass and MacDonald Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The MT DOT sensor at MacDonald Pass on US-12 milepost 27.9 at 6,325 ft. MSL reported a cold front passage around 1 am MST with light to moderate snowfall and winds sustained around 25 mph with gusts between 30 and 40 mph. MT DOT required chains for towing vehicles from 2 to 6:30 am MST at the pass due to blowing and drifting of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a very active pattern, the mountains along the Idaho and Montana continued to receive heavy snowfall which brought impacts to travel, especially along I-90 and US-12. A cold front brought strong gusty winds that brought limited visibility and hazardous driving conditions at Marias Pass and MacDonald Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very strong wind gusts nearing 50 mph occurred after a cold front went through during the early morning hours of Feb. 19 near Marias Pass. This combined with 4 to 6 inches of new transportable snow caused reduced visibility, blowing and drifting snow and severe driving conditions 7 miles west of Marias Pass to Marias Pass on US-2. There were reports of 3 foot drifts near Marias Pass and US-2 east of Marias pass was closed between 10 am and 2:30 pm MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and warm air mass brought freezing levels up to between 6,000 and 7,000 feet MSL on Feb. 27 and brought freezing rain to western Montana. Yet another arctic air mass pushed into western Montana and this time there was enough transportable snow to cause blizzard conditions in places. There were reports of severe drifting and severe driving conditions causing an unusually high amount of vehicle slide-offs and accidents. A rare urban avalanche took the life of a 68 year old woman and injured two others in a northern Missoula suburb.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Montana DOT reported freezing rain from Lolo Pass to Lolo on US-12 during the afternoon on Feb. 27. The Lolo Pass SNOTEL reported around 5 of total snowfall that mainly fell on Feb. 28.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet combined with a plume of moisture from subtropical origins, provided for high snow levels approximately near 7,000 feet, freezing rain in the valleys, slick mountain passes, heavy high mountain snow, avalanche releases and rapid snow melt with minor flooding in the Bitterroot Valley. Strong westerly winds were also felt across portions of Powell and Silver Bow County on Feb. 11.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Peterson Meadows SNOTEL at 7,200 ft. MSL, located 4.6 miles south-southwest of Georgetown Lake reported 5 of total snowfall. A weather spotter reported a period of heavy snow at 8:14 am MST while the Georgetown Lake MT DOT sensor indicated southwest winds gusting up to 34 mph at times.  Light freezing rain with a thin glaze of ice was reported by a weather spotter one mile west-southwest of Deer Lodge at 8:54 am MST on Feb. 13. The MT DOT also reported freezing rain from Deer Lodge to Warm Springs on Interstate 90 at 8:54 am MST on Feb. 13. The MT DOT sensor located 8 miles north of Avon on Highway 141 reported snow showers that helped to bring gusty winds to 58 mph at 10:28 am MST on Feb. 11, to 50 mph at 3:12 pm MST on Feb. 10 and to 52 mph at 2:57 am MST on Feb. 13. A brief heavy snow shower brought wind gusts as high as 47 mph at the Bert Mooney Airport south of Butte between 11:30 to 12 pm MST on Feb. 11.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm-up brought ice jams to the entire Yellowstone River Basin.  It impacted the lower Yellowstone River basin from Glendive to the confluence of the Missouri River starting on March 9th and continuing through March 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice moved into the Glendive area overnight on March 9-10.  The ice jammed in Glendive causing the water to rise over 8 feet in less than 15 minutes.  The jam caused water and ice to flow across the Marsh Road just southwest of town, and it also took out several power poles.   The ice jam further impacted the town as water and ice filled into the Toepke Park baseball/softball complex.  At least one 1,000 gallon propane tank was lifted from its base and floated down river.  The USGS gauge in Glendive peaked at 57.29 feet, but the ice caused a large area of low-land flooding downstream of the gauge for the next 36 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm-up brought ice jams to the entire Yellowstone River Basin.  It impacted the lower Yellowstone River basin from Glendive to the confluence of the Missouri River starting on March 9th and continuing through March 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A series of ice jams caused moderate flooding along the river in Richland County.   One farm house had water two feet over the basement window wells, and in the worst flood in 1979, it was only 2 inches over the window wells.  The ice caused rapid rises of water, and two volunteer firefighters from Savage, MT got caught as the ice came up behind them while checking the river after dark.  It took a rescue helicopter from Minot Air Force Base to come and rescue them off of the ice island they were stranded on for 5 hours.  Several propane tanks were reported to have been pulled off their stands and floated downstream.  There was a lot of farmland and low lying areas that experienced flooding all the way from the Dawson/Richland County line to the confluence with the Missouri River a few miles into North Dakota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist upper level wave brought a period of heavy snow early on Feb. 20 and as it moved through the region, very unstable conditions helped to form heavy snow bands. These snow bands lasted through Friday Feb. 21.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Granite County dispatch reported 4 of heavy snow in Drummond 4 hours ending at 7:13 am. MT DOT required chains for towing units on MacDonald Pass on US-12 from 6:10 am to 8:27 am MST. A SNOTEL located 11 miles north-northeast of Bearmouth reported 8 of snow with 0.50 snow water equivalent. Nine slide-offs were reported from Bearmouth to Drummond throughout the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and blowing snow causes hazardous travel across western Montana. Around a foot of snow falls along the I-90 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS employee reported heavy snowfall in Florence by noon on Feb. 23. Snow was falling in the Missoula area at this time in the form of small crystals, most likely due to the influence of the strong easterly winds breaking up the dendrites. It was apparent watching webcams and observations in the Stevensville and Florence area that weak northerly winds, a shallow arctic air mass and overrunning moist Pacific air allowed for the heaviest snowfall. A person from the public from Stevensville posted 13 of total snowfall on a local media's Facebook page. Other storm snow totals include: 12 at both Florence 3.7 SE and foothills near Victor, 9 Hamilton, 8.8 Corvallis 4.2 ENE, and 4.6 at the Missoula International Airport,. On Feb. 24, Missoula Valley Christian, Darby and Victor schools closed while the Charlo, Stevensville and Frenchtown schools were delayed for 2 hours due to the weather. Numerous motor vehicle accidents were reported especially between 2 and 6 pm MST on Feb. 23 when the heaviest snow fell in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very impressive arctic air mass set up over the region by the end of February. A very mild and moist system brought moderate to heavy snow to northwest Montana. Places like Eureka that typically experience a rain/snow shadow from the moist westerly events were able to receive more snow because the arctic air mass was so deep. The moisture content of the weather system, as measured by weather balloons upstream, came in at 181% of normal. This system helped to erode the cold air already in place in the valleys of west-central Montana, caused very slick roads due to freezing rain which led to school closures on March 3rd. This was one of the biggest snow events for the season  across northwest Montana and caused a few avalanches in a few places but did not threaten anyone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP Seeley Lake Ranger Station reported a storm total snow of 11.6 with 0.84 of precipitation. A trained spotter located one mile south of Condon estimated 11 of snow 24 hours ending at 9 am on Mar. 3.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist upper level wave brought a period of heavy snow early on Feb. 20 and as it moved through the region, very unstable conditions helped to form heavy snow bands. These snow bands lasted through Friday Feb. 21.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer located one mile north of Greenough measured 11 of snow due to the heavy snow bands. The COOP located in Seeley Lake reported 9 of total snowfall with 0.50 of precipitation. A Missoula County deputy reported driving through a foot of snow on MT-200 from Seeley Lake to Missoula around 7 am MST. Very slick driving conditions were experienced in the afternoon and evening hours on I-90 from Bonner to Bearmouth with numerous accidents reported by the MT DOT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and blowing snow causes hazardous travel across western Montana. Around a foot of snow falls along the I-90 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP at the Polson Kerr Dam reported a storm total snowfall of 8 with 0.30 of precipitation. Other snowfall amounts include: 7.5 Bigfork 2.6 SSE, 7.3 Ronan 4.1 NE, 6.5 Columbia Falls 7.7 S, and 6 Creston. Strong gusty winds brought blowing snow and reduced visibilities at Bad Rock Canyon. The St. Ignatius schools, Salish Kootenai College and Two Eagle River high school in Pablo closed on Feb. 24 due to the weather. The Arlee, Dixon and Charlo schools had a two hour delay. Multiple reports of vehicle accidents and slide-offs were reported especially in the Mission Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Many locations across west-central Montana were still trying to dig out from the snowfall that occurred over the previous few days. Before this storm started, deep ruts existed on secondary roads in the Missoula area and with the additional heavy snowfall, the impact became fairly high resulting in difficult travel. Strong ascent of warm moist air moving over the arctic air in place combined with weak winds in the low levels of the atmosphere allowed for the best snow crystal growth (large dendrites) and therefore heavy snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Montana DOT reported heavy snowfall with snow-covered roads during the evening hours of Feb. 24. They also required chains for towing units on Interstate 90 from St. Regis to Lookout Pass. It is a rare occasion for chains to be required from St. Regis to Saltese. A weather spotter 9 miles northeast of Thompson Falls measured 4 in 3 hours from 6 pm to 9:08 pm MST. Similarly, a weather spotter located at Evaro Hill measured 4 during that same time-frame.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near record snowfall for the month of February and strong easterly winds associated with an arctic air mass intrusion, caused severe wind loading on the western slopes of Mount Jumbo between Feb. 27-28. A snowboarder triggered a hard slab avalanche on a west facing, 35 degree slope of Mount Jumbo during the late afternoon of Feb. 28. The snowboarder was caught and carried for several yards before he was able to get out by himself. The avalanche traveled at approximately 80 to 100 mph and caught two children, ages 8 and 10, who were playing in their backyard as it slammed into and destroyed a two story wood frame home built in 1975. One child was partially buried  up to their armpits but not injured while the other was completely buried under 3 feet of snow. This child was later rescued, taken to the hospital and sustained only  minor injuries. The two residents of the destroyed home, a 66 year old male and his 68 year old wife were inside their home when it was hit and both were buried under several feet of snow and debris. Live power lines and broken natural gas lines complicated the rescue. The 66 year old male was rescued, had multiple injuries and lived. The 68 year old wife was rescued, but later died on March 3 from traumatic injuries. Three rental homes, 8 vehicles and an apartment building were also damaged by the avalanche. The avalanche width was 658 ft., depth was 2.5 to 4 ft. and the distance that it traveled from crown to terminus was 2,200 ft. Information courtesy of the West Central Montana Avalanche Center. The National Weather Service Missoula calculated a rough property damage estimate of $319K.

The Lindbergh Lake COOP saw 59.7 of snow for the month of February which breaks the previous all-time record of 47 set back in 1966. Records go back to Feb. 1960 for that station.

Freezing drizzle was reported again on the morning of Feb. 12 and freezing rain caused very slick secondary roads on Feb. 13 with enough impact that the Hamilton, Victor, Florence, Corvallis and Frenchtown school districts all had two hour delays.

Strong westerly winds were also felt across portions of Powell and Silver Bow County on Feb. 11.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of anomalous warmth with temperatures approaching 50 degrees F, rain and rapid snow-melt on frozen ground, caused lowland flooding across the Bitterroot Valley. Just before noon on Feb. 12, a National Weather Service employee reported water covering up to three-quarters of US-93 just south of Florence. They also reported ponding of water at the Florence IGA store parking lot and fire hall. More flooding was reported on Feb. 13 and the Ravalli County EM reported that home owners in Victor and Corvallis had already experienced ponding of water around their homes and water running down the streets in Victor. At least one homeowner did experience water in their basement.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An avalanche forecaster located in Essex reported a storm total snowfall of 16 with just over an inch of snow water equivalent. The West Glacier COOP measured a storm total snowfall of 13 with 1.01 of precipitation. A person from the public located 6 miles southwest of Marias Pass reported an estimated 2 per hour snowfall rates at 9:11 pm MST on Mar. 2. Montana DOT reported severe driving conditions from Blacktail to Marias Pass for a period due to the heavy snow. Wind chills in the beginning of the event were ranging between -30 to -40 F through the US-2 corridor as temperatures were below zero with winds gusting to 30 mph out of the easterly direction. Because of the heavy snow and strong winds, an avalanche was released one mile north of Blacktail covering the BNSF train tracks. Because of the elevated avalanche threat, train service was shut down for period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a very cold winter resulting in area rivers, streams and creeks freezing, early March began with some heavy snow followed by a rapid warm-up. This warm-up resulted in snowmelt and ice-jam flooding over area rivers, creeks and streams that continued into the middle of the month. While many areas experienced flooding, the locations that were most impacted by flooding included Park County, areas along the Musselshell River, areas around Pryor Creek, and the Otter Creek area in Rosebud County. Common impacts from the flooding included water over roadways and low-land flooding. There were also other areas that experienced flooding but impacts were minor. ||The following were the major impacts from the flooding:||- Gov. Steve Bullock declared a flood emergency for the state entire state due to heavy snowfall combined with warm temperatures causing rapid snow melt. |- The city of Livingston and Park County declared a state of emergency as officials responded to runoff flooding that started on Livingstons's north side. Some evacuations of residents occurred. |- The Montana Red Cross opened a shelter in Livingston for residents, who were evacuated from their homes due to flooding. The areas that experienced the most flooding were Livingston Ditch and Fleshman Creek. |- The city of Livingston declared a state of emergency. Temperatures quickly went from the below zero into the 40s which resulted in rapid snowmelt on the upper regions around Livingston. Fleshman Creek overflowed near Park Street resulting in about 70 people being evacuated from their homes. The Livingston Irrigation Ditch also overflowed in several places. ||Elsewhere, a helicopter was used for a flood rescue in Carbon County. An ice jam broke on the Clarks Fork River causing a massive flood resulting in a helicopter rescue of several people that were trapped by rising floodwaters. At least three people were safely rescued with no injuries. The rescue took place in an area near Byam Road and Highway 212. ||Significant impacts from flooding also occurred in the Ashland area in Rosebud County. Otter Creek washed out part of the East Tongue River Road just east of the Ashland Airport. Ice jams and snow melt caused the flooding, which took out three culverts and the dirt under the road. Crews added a walking bridge but flood waters washed that away. An old railroad car was put in place to use as a temporary bridge. Water also flooded nearby homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some low land flooding occurred across portions of Pryor Creek between the town of Pryor and the Interstate 90 bridge. Low land flooding was also reported along portions of Pryor Creek between the town of Pryor and its confluence with the Yellowstone River at Huntley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong jet stream moved over South Central Montana resulting in a few high wind gusts across portions of the Billings Forecast Area.

A strong upper level jet combined with a plume of moisture from subtropical origins, provided for high snow levels approximately near 7,000 feet, freezing rain in the valleys, slick mountain passes, heavy high mountain snow, avalanche releases and rapid snow melt with minor flooding in the Bitterroot Valley. Strong westerly winds were also felt across portions of Powell and Silver Bow County on Feb. 11.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A warm occlusion brought a transition from snow to rain during the morning hours of Feb. 12 to elevations below 7,000 feet. Strong warming aloft associated with a plume of moisture with subtropical origins most likely nixed the ability for the formation of stellar dendrites; therefore, heavy snow wasn't able to materialize in these areas as such is common with heavy up-slope westerly flow events. Nonetheless, moderate impact was experienced during the nighttime and morning hours as light to moderate snow made road surfaces slick. Both Lolo and Lost Trail Passes saw around 4 of total snowfall while total precipitation amounts ranged between 1 and 1.80 in the Bitterroot Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a very cold winter resulting in area rivers, streams and creeks freezing, early March began with some heavy snow followed by a rapid warm-up. This warm-up resulted in snowmelt and ice-jam flooding over area rivers, creeks and streams that continued into the middle of the month. While many areas experienced flooding, the locations that were most impacted by flooding included Park County, areas along the Musselshell River, areas around Pryor Creek, and the Otter Creek area in Rosebud County. Common impacts from the flooding included water over roadways and low-land flooding. There were also other areas that experienced flooding but impacts were minor. ||The following were the major impacts from the flooding:||- Gov. Steve Bullock declared a flood emergency for the state entire state due to heavy snowfall combined with warm temperatures causing rapid snow melt. |- The city of Livingston and Park County declared a state of emergency as officials responded to runoff flooding that started on Livingstons's north side. Some evacuations of residents occurred. |- The Montana Red Cross opened a shelter in Livingston for residents, who were evacuated from their homes due to flooding. The areas that experienced the most flooding were Livingston Ditch and Fleshman Creek. |- The city of Livingston declared a state of emergency. Temperatures quickly went from the below zero into the 40s which resulted in rapid snowmelt on the upper regions around Livingston. Fleshman Creek overflowed near Park Street resulting in about 70 people being evacuated from their homes. The Livingston Irrigation Ditch also overflowed in several places. ||Elsewhere, a helicopter was used for a flood rescue in Carbon County. An ice jam broke on the Clarks Fork River causing a massive flood resulting in a helicopter rescue of several people that were trapped by rising floodwaters. At least three people were safely rescued with no injuries. The rescue took place in an area near Byam Road and Highway 212. ||Significant impacts from flooding also occurred in the Ashland area in Rosebud County. Otter Creek washed out part of the East Tongue River Road just east of the Ashland Airport. Ice jams and snow melt caused the flooding, which took out three culverts and the dirt under the road. Crews added a walking bridge but flood waters washed that away. An old railroad car was put in place to use as a temporary bridge. Water also flooded nearby homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County officials reported low land and small stream flooding impacting portions of Musselshell County...mainly small creeks emerging from the Snowy and Bull Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very impressive arctic air mass set up over the region by the end of February. A very mild and moist system brought moderate to heavy snow to northwest Montana. Places like Eureka that typically experience a rain/snow shadow from the moist westerly events were able to receive more snow because the arctic air mass was so deep. The moisture content of the weather system, as measured by weather balloons upstream, came in at 181% of normal. This system helped to erode the cold air already in place in the valleys of west-central Montana, caused very slick roads due to freezing rain which led to school closures on March 3rd. This was one of the biggest snow events for the season  across northwest Montana and caused a few avalanches in a few places but did not threaten anyone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lookout Pass Ski resort reported 17 of heavy snow in 24 hours. Montana DOT required chains for towing units over Lookout Pass and labeled the I-90 stretch from St. Regis to Lookout Pass severe driving conditions due to heavy snow, poor visibility and icy roadways. Six snow slide-offs were reported between mile-markers 10 and 33 on I-90 by the afternoon of Mar. 3 which closed the westbound lanes from 3 to 5 pm MST. All east and westbound lanes were closed due to avalanche danger at 5:47 pm and traffic was re-routed to Hwy-200 until 4 pm MST on Mar. 4. Thirty vehicle incidents were reported by the MT DOJ in the two day period; 9 were crashes, 13 were slide-offs and 8 were stranded vehicles. Other snowfall amounts include: 18 Haugan 1 W, 10.7 Heron 2 NW, 10 Thompson Falls and 5 Alberton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of very warm temperatures ranging from 50 to 60 degrees occurred after an above normal snowfall winter across western Montana. This combined with moderate to heavy rainfall and high snow levels resulted in a quick melt of snowpack in various locations. Impacts felt included road closures due to avalanche danger, flooding of the Little Bitterroot River, rock slides across northwest Montana and lowland flooding across the Bitterroot Valley. President Obama declared a major disaster in the state of Montana which included Sanders, Lake and Ravalli Counties to help with the flood cleanup costs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several days of moderate to heavy rainfall contributed to rock slides across northwest Montana. One in particular blocked a lane on Montana 37 north of the Libby Dam for a few hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Glacier Park International Airport reported a storm total snowfall of 10 with 0.47 of precipitation. Other snowfall totals include: 7.5 Whitefish, 7 Columbia Falls 1 NW, 6.3 Bigfork 13 S, 8 Polson Kerr Dam and 6.5 Polson. A weather spotter located in Columbia Falls reported snow-covered roads and were pretty bad during the afternoon of Mar. 2. An above normal amount of vehicle slide-offs and accidents were reported during this event from the Flathead to the Mission Valleys; 20 were crashes, 22 were slide-offs and 2 were stranded vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a continuation of impacts from the blizzard conditions that occurred on Feb. 28, extreme wind chill values mainly ranged between 20 to 30 below zero Fahrenheit across western Montana. A few areas along the Continental Divide, especially in constrictions that caused wind channeling, were able to get as cold or colder than -40 F during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station located 4 miles north of Kila reported a wind chill of -26 deg. F at 8:08 am MST on Mar. 1. Another station located one mile east-southeast of Marion reported -23 deg. F at 4:48 am MST on Mar. 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of snowfall fell across the higher elevations of southwest Montana. An avalanche takes the life of a skier near Philipsburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 27 year old male died as a result of asphyxia after being buried by a hard slab avalanche on a westerly slope above Altoona Lakes, in the Flint Creek Range or about 11 miles east-northeast of Philipsburg.  The crown was located at 8,512 feet, slope angle was 35 degrees plus, length was 1,324 feet on the ground and the estimated horizontal length of the crown was 2,100 feet. An investigation made by the West Central Montana Avalanche Center found that dry & cold weather during the early winter and a shallow snowpack had created a very weak faceted layer near the ground was 10 inches in depth. The skier broke through the weakness near some rocks which allowed for the season's snowpack to slide at once.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of snowfall fell across the higher elevations of southwest Montana. An avalanche takes the life of a skier near Philipsburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter located 7.4 northwest of Anaconda reported 6.5 of snowfall in 18 hours.

President Obama declared a major disaster in the state of Montana which included Sanders, Lake and Ravalli Counties to help with the flood cleanup costs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to the large amount of precipitation between 1 and 2 inches in the mountains with high freezing levels, natural avalanche releases were reported between Essex and Marias Pass. A BNSF avalanche forecaster reported very large avalanche releases during the early morning hours on March 6. The MT DOT reported that US-2 between Essex and 7 miles west of Marias Pass was closed from 2 to 4:30 MST on March 6 due to avalanche mitigation. Both freight and passenger train service was shut down for an estimated 24 hour period between March 6-7.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A driveway was completely washed out by a flood, presumably caused by snowmelt and very warm temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow zone of unstable air and a break in the clouds allowed brief daytime heating to generate a line of severe thunderstorms during the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public estimated a 60 mph wind gust while driving along Highway 24 between Fort Peck and Glasgow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of very warm temperatures ranging from 50 to 60 degrees occurred after an above normal snowfall winter across western Montana. This combined with moderate to heavy rainfall and high snow levels resulted in a quick melt of snowpack in various locations. Impacts felt included road closures due to avalanche danger, flooding of the Little Bitterroot River, rock slides across northwest Montana and lowland flooding across the Bitterroot Valley. President Obama declared a major disaster in the state of Montana which included Sanders, Lake and Ravalli Counties to help with the flood cleanup costs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Above normal snowfall in February resulted in an unusually high snowpack across the Bitterroot Valley at the beginning of March. The Hamilton COOP reported a snow depth of 16 on March 2 which disappeared by March 9. Weather spotters in the Florence area reported well over a foot of snow at the beginning of March. This snowpack combined with 0.50 to 1 of mild rainfall and temperatures well into the 50's caused rapid snowmelt and subsequently overland flooding in the Bitterroot Valley. A person from the public in Florence off Hidden Valley Rd. reported a damaged fence, multiple homes with water damage and several windows broken. The MT DOT reported heavy water on the roadways S-203 2 miles east of Florence, near the junction with US-93. They also reported heavy amounts of water on S-203 10 miles north of the Stevensville Junction with S-269. The Ravalli County DES coordinator remarked that this was the worst flooding he had seen in 41 years in these areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While a surface low tracked northeast into British Columbia during the morning of February 14th, moist and relatively milder air streamed over Idaho and western Montana. The warm front brought brief periods of intense snowfall across much of the area causing low visibilities for a brief time and slick roadways. After the warm front moved through, the air-mass became unstable with strong westerly winds aloft. This helped to form fairly organized graupel, snow and rain bands, some of which brought moderate impacts to area mountain passes. A ridge of high pressure moved in at night causing temperatures to cool and black ice to form.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slush and snow-covered roads were reported across much of Lincoln County during the morning into the afternoon hours on Feb. 14. A person from the public located in Olney reported 2 of heavy snow two hours ending at 10:49 MST. Whitefish Mountain Ski Resort reported a total of 3 at their base elevation 4,464 ft. 24 hours ending 5:50 am MST on Feb. 15.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a continuation of impacts from the blizzard conditions that occurred on Feb. 28, extreme wind chill values mainly ranged between 20 to 30 below zero Fahrenheit across western Montana. A few areas along the Continental Divide, especially in constrictions that caused wind channeling, were able to get as cold or colder than -40 F during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fielding RAWS station located 4.4 miles southwest of Marias Pass reported extreme wind chill values that peaked around -40 deg. F during the evening hours of Mar. 1. The MT DOT near Essex reported a wind chill of -35 F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a continuation of impacts from the blizzard conditions that occurred on Feb. 28, extreme wind chill values mainly ranged between 20 to 30 below zero Fahrenheit across western Montana. A few areas along the Continental Divide, especially in constrictions that caused wind channeling, were able to get as cold or colder than -40 F during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Glacier National Airport located 8 miles north of Kalispell reported wind chill values between -20 and -30 deg. F while winds were ranging between 10 and 31 mph mainly blowing out of Bad Rock Canyon.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Missoula International Airport reported the coldest wind chill value of -24 deg. F at 9:09 am MST on Mar. 1. Winds were gusting as high as 35 mph at the airport, but were probably over 50 mph near Hellgate Canyon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a continuation of impacts from the blizzard conditions that occurred on Feb. 28, extreme wind chill values mainly ranged between 20 to 30 below zero Fahrenheit across western Montana. A few areas along the Continental Divide, especially in constrictions that caused wind channeling, were able to get as cold or colder than -40 F during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The MT DOT sensor located near Helmville reported a wind chill of -41 deg. F at 8:06 am on Mar. 1. Other cold wind chill values include: -40 Garrison, -37 Elliston and -32 at both Georgetown Lake and Bert Mooney Airport near Butte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a very cold winter resulting in area rivers, streams and creeks freezing, early March began with some heavy snow followed by a rapid warm-up. This warm-up resulted in snowmelt and ice-jam flooding over area rivers, creeks and streams that continued into the middle of the month. While many areas experienced flooding, the locations that were most impacted by flooding included Park County, areas along the Musselshell River, areas around Pryor Creek, and the Otter Creek area in Rosebud County. Common impacts from the flooding included water over roadways and low-land flooding. There were also other areas that experienced flooding but impacts were minor. ||The following were the major impacts from the flooding:||- Gov. Steve Bullock declared a flood emergency for the state entire state due to heavy snowfall combined with warm temperatures causing rapid snow melt. |- The city of Livingston and Park County declared a state of emergency as officials responded to runoff flooding that started on Livingstons's north side. Some evacuations of residents occurred. |- The Montana Red Cross opened a shelter in Livingston for residents, who were evacuated from their homes due to flooding. The areas that experienced the most flooding were Livingston Ditch and Fleshman Creek. |- The city of Livingston declared a state of emergency. Temperatures quickly went from the below zero into the 40s which resulted in rapid snowmelt on the upper regions around Livingston. Fleshman Creek overflowed near Park Street resulting in about 70 people being evacuated from their homes. The Livingston Irrigation Ditch also overflowed in several places. ||Elsewhere, a helicopter was used for a flood rescue in Carbon County. An ice jam broke on the Clarks Fork River causing a massive flood resulting in a helicopter rescue of several people that were trapped by rising floodwaters. At least three people were safely rescued with no injuries. The rescue took place in an area near Byam Road and Highway 212. ||Significant impacts from flooding also occurred in the Ashland area in Rosebud County. Otter Creek washed out part of the East Tongue River Road just east of the Ashland Airport. Ice jams and snow melt caused the flooding, which took out three culverts and the dirt under the road. Crews added a walking bridge but flood waters washed that away. An old railroad car was put in place to use as a temporary bridge. Water also flooded nearby homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County officials reported that some streams and creeks were running high, and in some areas, water was running over roadways and flooding fields. Some roads became impassable.

In addition, some county roads were reported to be impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Many locations across west-central Montana were still trying to dig out from the snowfall that occurred over the previous few days. Before this storm started, deep ruts existed on secondary roads in the Missoula area and with the additional heavy snowfall, the impact became fairly high resulting in difficult travel. Strong ascent of warm moist air moving over the arctic air in place combined with weak winds in the low levels of the atmosphere allowed for the best snow crystal growth (large dendrites) and therefore heavy snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Missoula International Airport received 5.5 of snow in 5 hours, but received a total of 6.8 from this particular storm event with 0.29 of precipitation. The heavy snow fell during the later part of rush hour traffic and the secondary roads became congested with many vehicles being stuck in deep ruts. The main roads were being plowed, but the melted snow from earlier had frozen into a sheet of ice exacerbating road conditions. Many school systems in the Missoula and Bitterroot Valleys had delays on the morning of Feb. 26.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and warm air mass brought freezing levels up to between 6,000 and 7,000 feet MSL on Feb. 27 and brought freezing rain to western Montana. Yet another arctic air mass pushed into western Montana and this time there was enough transportable snow to cause blizzard conditions in places. There were reports of severe drifting and severe driving conditions causing an unusually high amount of vehicle slide-offs and accidents. A rare urban avalanche took the life of a 68 year old woman and injured two others in a northern Missoula suburb.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported whiteout conditions 2 miles west-southwest of Columbia Falls. Winds were gusting to 50 mph during the event. Their best estimation of the snowfall was 4 inches. Snow bands developed during the day and brought the heaviest snow to the Ronan area where a storm total of 8.5 fell as measured by a weather spotter. The Columbia Falls schools closed on Feb. 28 due to the weather. A weather spotter located 8 miles east-southeast of Rollins reported a wind gust of 53 mph at 10 pm MST on Feb. 28. The Northwestern A divisional basketball tournament in Kalispell was postponed as well. As a side note, freezing rain fell on Feb. 27 between 1 and 4 pm MST from Arlee to Ronan causing slick conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a continuation of impacts from the blizzard conditions that occurred on Feb. 28, extreme wind chill values mainly ranged between 20 to 30 below zero Fahrenheit across western Montana. A few areas along the Continental Divide, especially in constrictions that caused wind channeling, were able to get as cold or colder than -40 F during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 12:33 pm MST on Mar. 1, a local newspaper reported that a large tree blew onto a car near the intersection of Cooper and Scott Streets in Missoula due to the wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a continuation of impacts from the blizzard conditions that occurred on Feb. 28, extreme wind chill values mainly ranged between 20 to 30 below zero Fahrenheit across western Montana. A few areas along the Continental Divide, especially in constrictions that caused wind channeling, were able to get as cold or colder than -40 F during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather sensor located 4 miles east of Mullan reported the coldest wind chill of -21 deg. F. A weather sensor located three miles north of Ninemile reported -20 deg. F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just before midnight on January 11, precipitation developed in the higher elevations along the border of Idaho and Montana as milder and relatively moist air pushed into the region ahead of a strong Pacific storm system. An upper level trough with an above normal moisture content from subtropical origins combined with a strong upper level jet tracked through the Northern Rockies from January 11-12. This system brought heavy precipitation in the form of mountain snow & blowing snow, valley mixed precipitation including freezing rain due to below freezing ground temperatures across northwest Montana, high winds, wind loading of snow on eastern aspects in higher elevations elevating the avalanche risk and the risk for rock slides. Snow bands brought more snow to mountain passes and valley locations alike as the trough exited the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the strong cold front passed through around 1 pm MST on Jan. 11, wind gusts between 45 and 52 mph were felt near populations centers such as Kila and Libby. At 3:06 pm MST, the Zonolite RAWS sensor located at 4,204 ft., 8 miles east-northeast of Libby, recorded a wind gust to 65 mph. The Blacktail Ski Area reported very strong winds that blew down trees across power lines on their access road. Fire personnel responded to several downed trees on power lines between Marion and Kila by 4 pm. MST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The HADS sensor at Marias Pass reported a total storm precipitation of 0.67 which could have equated to around 6 inches of snow, matching the nearby SNOTEL measurements. A NWS employee reported blowing snow with approximately 5 of snow on the ground as they drove through Marias Pass around 1 pm MST on Jan. 12. An avalanche forecaster reported just under an inch of precipitation mostly in the form of rain. They also reported two avalanches that occurred from natural triggers, but did not affect US-2. The Fielding RAWS sensor recorded a wind gust of 51 mph at 2:42 pm MST on Jan. 11. Other precipitation amounts include: 1.27 Hungry Horse Dam COOP and 0.42 West Glacier COOP.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lookout Pass Ski area reported 10 of snow that fell in 11 hours through 6:25 am MST Jan. 12. The MT DOT sensor at Lookout Pass reported wind gusts between 25 and 39 mph from 8 pm to 11:26 pm Jan. 11. The strong winds combined with moderate to heavy snow, most likely caused blowing snow which worsened visibilities. An automobile accident occurred at Lookout Pass around 7:35 pm MST and closed all lanes in both directions on Interstate 90 for some time.

Snow bands brought more snow to mountain passes and valley locations alike as the trough exited the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed south and east across the Stevensville area around 2 pm MST and wind gusts were close to 30 mph. Radar echoes revealed showers behind the front which affected the area for an hour afterwards. A National Weather Service employee observed a mountain wave developing behind the cold front across the northern Bitterroot Valley. A person from the public located 6 miles south of Lolo, recorded 88 mph on their home anemometer sometime between 2:30 and 3:15 pm MST. They reported roof shingles blown off their roof and a tree limb fell on their house. Three other homes in that area also saw shingle damage and a few trees were blown down. A weather spotter located 2 miles west-northwest of Stevensville estimated that a 60 mph wind gust occurred at 2:57 pm MST. They reported shingle damage and the top of a tree blew off. The wind gust occurred simultaneously with a rain and snow shower.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist southwest flow ahead of a cold front brought a period of heavy snow to Lookout Pass while several valley locations saw several inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lookout Pass SNOTEL reported 0.10 or more SWE (snow water equivalent) per hour through this period. Web cams confirmed that snow was falling at a good clip and the MT DOT required chains on towing units from 7:52 am to 9:28 pm MST. The SNOTEL reported a total of 1.50 of SWE which could have equated to over a foot of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist southwest flow ahead of a cold front brought a period of heavy snow to Lookout Pass while several valley locations saw several inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lolo Pass SNOTEL reported 7 of total snowfall with 0.80 of snow water equivalent while the Saddle Mountain SNOTEL near Lost Trail Pass measured 5 of snow with 0.30 of liquid. The MT DOT required that towing units have chains at Lolo Pass during the evening of Feb. 17.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist southwest flow ahead of a cold front brought a period of heavy snow to Lookout Pass while several valley locations saw several inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Glacier Park International Airport reported a total snowfall of 1.8 inches that caused roads to become snow covered and icy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and warm air mass brought freezing levels up to between 6,000 and 7,000 feet MSL on Feb. 27 and brought freezing rain to western Montana. Yet another arctic air mass pushed into western Montana and this time there was enough transportable snow to cause blizzard conditions in places. There were reports of severe drifting and severe driving conditions causing an unusually high amount of vehicle slide-offs and accidents. A rare urban avalanche took the life of a 68 year old woman and injured two others in a northern Missoula suburb.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person from the public reported whiteout conditions just to the west of Hellgate Canyon where they estimated wind gusts to 50 mph at times. They had a tough time driving the streets in their four wheel drive vehicle. There were 1 to 2 foot drifts on the streets with up to 9 foot drifts in some parking lots. Numerous businesses delayed  opening, and several closed for the day as employees could not make it to work.  Many vehicles became stuck and were temporarily abandoned in downtown streets.  All public and private schools in Missoula were canceled. The Missoula International Airport storm total snowfall was 9.1 with 0.70 of precipitation. Freezing rain was reported on Feb. 27 from Hamilton to Missoula before it changed to snow by the evening hours. It caused very slick conditions in places, especially side streets. There were numerous motor vehicle slide-offs and accidents reported throughout the event. The Missoula snowfall total for the month of February was 40.2 and became the 2nd highest on record since 1894.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS employee observed a small stream that had ran over the Old East Highway northwest of Florence. The combination of rain and snow melt and a cold ground, created conditions for the overland flooding.

This system brought heavy precipitation in the form of mountain snow & blowing snow, valley mixed precipitation including freezing rain due to below freezing ground temperatures across northwest Montana, high winds, wind loading of snow on eastern aspects in higher elevations elevating the avalanche risk and the risk for rock slides. Snow bands brought more snow to mountain passes and valley locations alike as the trough exited the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The sheriff in Hamilton reported up to 2 of snow fell associated with snow bands. Other reports include: 1.5 Florence and 4 in Grantsdale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm, moist, and unstable atmosphere was undercut by a low pressure system and its associated cold front from the west which generated a widespread area of organized severe weather, impacting Phillips and Dawson Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter measured a 72 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of Pacific moisture along with an upper low brought heavy snow to portions of the Billings Forecast Area. In addition, a surface low across the Dakotas resulted in tight pressure gradients, strong winds and blizzard conditions to portions of far Southeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 6 inches were reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and moderately unstable air mass ahead of a convectively-induced cyclonic circulation was in place across Eastern Montana.  Dewpoints in the middle to upper 50s with MLCAPES around 1500J/kg combined with sufficient vertical wind shear resulted in severe thunderstorms across the eastern portions of the Billings Forecast area. These thunderstorms produced large hail and a couple of brief tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief rope tornado touched down in an open field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On May 24, the NRCS SNOTEL snow water equivalent index for the Bitterroot River Basin was 204% of normal while the Upper Clark Fork Basin saw 165% of normal.  This above normal amount of water in the snowpack combined with above normal temperatures contributed to minor flooding along the Bitterroot and Clark Fork Rivers in the last two weeks of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS Bitterroot River gauge at Missoula reported a peak height of 11.11 feet (11 ft. is the minor flood stage) and peak discharge rate of 17,300 cubic feet per second at 8:30 pm MDT on May 25. A NWS employee reported field flooding between Lolo and Missoula.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a very cold winter resulting in area rivers, streams and creeks freezing, early March began with some heavy snow followed by a rapid warm-up. This warm-up resulted in snowmelt and ice-jam flooding over area rivers, creeks and streams that continued into the middle of the month. While many areas experienced flooding, the locations that were most impacted by flooding included Park County, areas along the Musselshell River, areas around Pryor Creek, and the Otter Creek area in Rosebud County. Common impacts from the flooding included water over roadways and low-land flooding. There were also other areas that experienced flooding but impacts were minor. ||The following were the major impacts from the flooding:||- Gov. Steve Bullock declared a flood emergency for the state entire state due to heavy snowfall combined with warm temperatures causing rapid snow melt. |- The city of Livingston and Park County declared a state of emergency as officials responded to runoff flooding that started on Livingstons's north side. Some evacuations of residents occurred. |- The Montana Red Cross opened a shelter in Livingston for residents, who were evacuated from their homes due to flooding. The areas that experienced the most flooding were Livingston Ditch and Fleshman Creek. |- The city of Livingston declared a state of emergency. Temperatures quickly went from the below zero into the 40s which resulted in rapid snowmelt on the upper regions around Livingston. Fleshman Creek overflowed near Park Street resulting in about 70 people being evacuated from their homes. The Livingston Irrigation Ditch also overflowed in several places. ||Elsewhere, a helicopter was used for a flood rescue in Carbon County. An ice jam broke on the Clarks Fork River causing a massive flood resulting in a helicopter rescue of several people that were trapped by rising floodwaters. At least three people were safely rescued with no injuries. The rescue took place in an area near Byam Road and Highway 212. ||Significant impacts from flooding also occurred in the Ashland area in Rosebud County. Otter Creek washed out part of the East Tongue River Road just east of the Ashland Airport. Ice jams and snow melt caused the flooding, which took out three culverts and the dirt under the road. Crews added a walking bridge but flood waters washed that away. An old railroad car was put in place to use as a temporary bridge. Water also flooded nearby homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County officials and the Montana Department of Transportation reported small stream and low land flooding. Numerous roads and some highways were reported to have water flowing across them.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail didn't cover the entire ground, but there was a lot of hail from half an inch to one and a half inches across at least a three mile area.  Some minor vehicle damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A generally unsettled pattern persisted through this period. Lingering low level moisture, daytime heating, and weak disturbances moving through the moist westerly flow aloft, brought scattered showers with isolated thunderstorms to portions of South-Central and Southwest Montana. Hail and gusty winds were reported from some storms, and a few cells became severe. Showers and storms generally decreased each night as conditions stabilized.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail measuring 1.75 inches was reported in Sun Prairie, MT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance moved through Montana with increasing instability and moisture combining to allow for evening convective development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail measuring 1.00 inches (quarter sized) was reported about 3 mi N of Fairfield, MT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance moved through Montana with increasing instability and moisture combining to allow for evening convective development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of about 1.00 inch in 30 minutes reported with passing thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave and upper level jet energy moved over Montana through the period as a weak NW-SE frontal boundary remained stalled across the forecast area. Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms, some severe, developed late afternoon and evening over North-Central Montana, with activity diminishing during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail 1.00 inches (quarter sized) reported about 7 mi SE of Rocky Boy, MT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very unstable conditions contributed to thunderstorm development northwest of Kalispell which allowed for a funnel cloud to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An official observer at the Glacier Park International Airport observed a funnel cloud approximately 8 miles to the northwest. They reported that it didn't seem to reach the ground and only lasted for a minute. Radar and lightning data confirmed that the funnel cloud was attached to a cumulonimbus cloud(thunderstorm). Due to strong deep layer southwest wind shear ranging between 40 and 45 knots and with the relative location of the precipitation shaft to the base of the storm, the thunderstorm could have been supercellular. The Glacier National Park ASOS observed high relative humidities and south to south-southeast winds between 13 and 17 knots which added low-level wind shear. The appearance of the thunderstorm cloud base was relatively low which is another favorable atmospheric condition for funnel cloud development. Thanks to James LaDue from the NWS Warning Decision Training Branch for his added contribution to this report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure system moved across the Pacific Northwest bringing a period of widespread precipitation and below average temperatures. Rain was seen over the lower elevations with snow in some of the higher terrain. The heaviest precipitation was seen along eastern slopes of the Continental Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of about 1.5 inches since midnight was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved east through Central Montana keeping the region under the influence of both disturbed flow to the west and an approaching trough from the north. Surface low pressure to the east and building high pressure to the west kept brisk, westerly surface winds across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind gusts, peaking at 83KT (95MPH), were reported 13 mi WSW of Saint Mary, MT at the Logan Pass Visitor's Center sensor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The seasons first severe weather outbreak occurred during the afternoon and evening hours of the 18th. A cluster of thunderstorms developed across Sweet Grass and Stillwater Counties during the early afternoon hours as an upper level impulse approached the area. As the line moved east into a more favorable buoyancy/shear environment, (MLCAPE combined with 500 to 1500J/KG along with effective shears of 55 to 60kts) the thunderstorms quickly intensified, especially over the Billings area. ||Three large feeder-lines went down leaving 2,600 customers in Laurel and the West End of Billings without power for about an hour. In addition, the large hail, some of which was the size of walnuts and a few tennis ball size, resulted in considerable damage to homes and cars across the immediate Billings area. ||As for area crops, the hail mowed a 3,000 acre patch across sugar beet farms from Billings to Hardin. Winter wheat, 64 percent of which had been rated good to excellent, took a beating, as did malt barley. Sugar beets seemed to get the worst of the damage. The heaviest damage was found north of Hardin, west of Billings and north of Worden/Shepherd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 60 mph was estimated just west of Ballantine.

The snowpack during most of the month of May was around 150% of average. There was significant melting of the snowpack below 8000 feet during the month. The melting snowpack resulted in rising river levels and river flooding. The main impacts of the high river flows were fast flows, low-land flooding and bank erosion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Park County DES reported that there was erosion damage to the rip-rap levee on the north side of the Yellowstone River, approximately 1000 feet south of the Park County High School in the city of Livingston. An area 40' long eroded into 10' into the levee. The levee eroded parallel to the river to a length of 100' along the north bank.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm produced a long-lived tornado across parts of East Central Carter County during the afternoon of June 17th. Eye-witnesses reported that the tornado was on the ground for approximately one hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm developed very quickly in Eastern Carter County around 2:15 pm. The thunderstorm produced the first ever EF-3 rated tornado in southern Montana. The tornado first touched down at 2:27 pm around 13 miles southwest of Camp Crook, South Dakota, or about 10 miles southwest of Capitol, Montana. The tornado formed in the Sheep Mountains and eventually crossed Tie Creek Road as it moved slowly north. People lost site of the tornado as it entered Custer National Forest around 3:30 pm. ||The tornado was on the ground for an hour and traveled 10 miles and was reported to be a half mile wide at times. The tornado completely destroyed a trailer home, twisting and throwing debris over a mile. A small and old A-frame school house was completely destroyed, with debris blown 100 yards away, leaving only the basement behind. Six vehicles were picked up and tossed up to 200 yards. Around 20 power poles were snapped off with one pulled up out of the ground. In addition, 20 hay bales, weighing 3/4 ton a piece, were blown so far away that they were not recovered. A swath of trees on the north end of the track into the Custer National Forest was flattened by the tornado. There were no injuries or deaths from the tornado but two horses were killed. ||This was the strongest tornado ever recorded in the Billings National Weather Service area of responsibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A group of seven snowmobilers was riding north of Cooke City when an 18 year old from Minnesota was caught in an avalanche on a steep, northwest facing slope on Crown Butte outside Cooke City. The victim was stopped on the slope while two others were riding on it when the avalanche was triggered. The others rode off, but the victim was caught and buried. Neither he, nor anyone in his party of seven, was wearing a beacon. He was fully buried and Cooke City Search and Rescue located him with a probe pole under six feet of debris near the toe of the avalanche about 1.5-2 hours later.||The avalanche broke 700 wide and 2-5 feet deep on depth hoar crystals about 1 foot above the ground. The avalanche ran 800 feet vertical and 1600 feet slope distance. Debris ranged from 5 to 15 feet deep and covered an area 900 by 400 feet. Slope angles in the starting zone ranged from 36 to 38 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough that moved across the Continental Divide combined with large scale ascent...deep-layer vertical shear in the 30-40kt range which supported updraft organization and persistence...resulted in the development of severe thunderstorms across Southeast Montana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The seasons first severe weather outbreak occurred during the afternoon and evening hours of the 18th. A cluster of thunderstorms developed across Sweet Grass and Stillwater Counties during the early afternoon hours as an upper level impulse approached the area. As the line moved east into a more favorable buoyancy/shear environment, (MLCAPE combined with 500 to 1500J/KG along with effective shears of 55 to 60kts) the thunderstorms quickly intensified, especially over the Billings area. ||Three large feeder-lines went down leaving 2,600 customers in Laurel and the West End of Billings without power for about an hour. In addition, the large hail, some of which was the size of walnuts and a few tennis ball size, resulted in considerable damage to homes and cars across the immediate Billings area. ||As for area crops, the hail mowed a 3,000 acre patch across sugar beet farms from Billings to Hardin. Winter wheat, 64 percent of which had been rated good to excellent, took a beating, as did malt barley. Sugar beets seemed to get the worst of the damage. The heaviest damage was found north of Hardin, west of Billings and north of Worden/Shepherd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 60 mph was estimated just northwest of Billings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure system moved across the Pacific Northwest bringing a period of widespread precipitation and below average temperatures. Rain was seen over the lower elevations with snow in some of the higher terrain. The heaviest precipitation was seen along eastern slopes of the Continental Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of about 1.59 inches since midnight was reported at the Browning RAWS.

After a very cold winter resulting in area rivers, streams and creeks freezing, early March began with some heavy snow followed by a rapid warm-up. This warm-up resulted in snowmelt and ice-jam flooding over area rivers, creeks and streams that continued into the middle of the month. While many areas experienced flooding, the locations that were most impacted by flooding included Park County, areas along the Musselshell River, areas around Pryor Creek, and the Otter Creek area in Rosebud County. Common impacts from the flooding included water over roadways and low-land flooding. There were also other areas that experienced flooding but impacts were minor. ||The following were the major impacts from the flooding:||- Gov. Steve Bullock declared a flood emergency for the state entire state due to heavy snowfall combined with warm temperatures causing rapid snow melt. |- The city of Livingston and Park County declared a state of emergency as officials responded to runoff flooding that started on Livingstons's north side. Some evacuations of residents occurred. |- The Montana Red Cross opened a shelter in Livingston for residents, who were evacuated from their homes due to flooding. The areas that experienced the most flooding were Livingston Ditch and Fleshman Creek. |- The city of Livingston declared a state of emergency. Temperatures quickly went from the below zero into the 40s which resulted in rapid snowmelt on the upper regions around Livingston. Fleshman Creek overflowed near Park Street resulting in about 70 people being evacuated from their homes. The Livingston Irrigation Ditch also overflowed in several places. ||Elsewhere, a helicopter was used for a flood rescue in Carbon County. An ice jam broke on the Clarks Fork River causing a massive flood resulting in a helicopter rescue of several people that were trapped by rising floodwaters. At least three people were safely rescued with no injuries. The rescue took place in an area near Byam Road and Highway 212. ||Significant impacts from flooding also occurred in the Ashland area in Rosebud County. Otter Creek washed out part of the East Tongue River Road just east of the Ashland Airport. Ice jams and snow melt caused the flooding, which took out three culverts and the dirt under the road. Crews added a walking bridge but flood waters washed that away. An old railroad car was put in place to use as a temporary bridge. Water also flooded nearby homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding as a result of an ice jam on the Yellowstone River was reported a few miles east of Hysham along Gibson and Van Olsen Roads. Some farm structures were damaged by the high water.

On May 24, the NRCS SNOTEL snow water equivalent index for the Bitterroot River Basin was 204% of normal while the Upper Clark Fork Basin saw 165% of normal.  This above normal amount of water in the snowpack combined with above normal temperatures contributed to minor flooding along the Bitterroot and Clark Fork Rivers in the last two weeks of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS Bitterroot River gauge at Darby reported 5 instances where the river height surpassed the 7.50 ft. flood-stage: 7.84 ft. 8 am MDT 5/24, 7.57 ft. 8 am MDT 5/26, 7.76 ft. 815 am MDT 5/27, 7.63 ft. 8 am MDT 5/28 & 7.67 ft. 815 am MDT 5/29. It also reported a peak discharge rate of 8,590 cubic feet per second at 915 am MDT 5/24. Field flooding was reported by a NWS employee between Lolo and Florence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On May 24, the NRCS SNOTEL snow water equivalent index for the Bitterroot River Basin was 204% of normal while the Upper Clark Fork Basin saw 165% of normal.  This above normal amount of water in the snowpack combined with above normal temperatures contributed to minor flooding along the Bitterroot and Clark Fork Rivers in the last two weeks of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS Bitterroot River gauge at Darby reported a peak discharge rate of 8,590 cubic feet per second at 915 am MDT 5/24 and a peak height of 7.84 feet (7.5 ft. is the minor flood stage) at 8 am MDT 5/24. A gravel county road, Ross Lane, located just to the west of the Bitterroot River in Florence experienced minor flooding. The fishing access in Florence was totally inundated but was also closed due to construction. Also a few fields were flooded between Florence and Lolo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Diffluent southwest flow ahead of a trough brought instability and thunderstorms to Central Montana during the afternoon. East surface winds brought higher dew points to the area. The main threats from these storms were gusty winds and small hail. Convective activity decreased after midnight as the trough axis moved east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 1.25 inches was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail measuring 1.00 inches (quarter sized) was reported 1mi S of Eddies Corner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Favorable upper-level winds combined with marginal instability and moderatea surface moisture to produce a few severe thunderstorms across portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported hail stone sizes ranging from quarters to ping pong balls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved east through Central Montana keeping the region under the influence of both disturbed flow to the west and an approaching trough from the north. Surface low pressure to the east and building high pressure to the west kept brisk, westerly surface winds across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind gusts, peaking at 50KT (58MPH), were reported 1 mi NE of Belgrade, MT at the Bozeman Airport ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The seasons first severe weather outbreak occurred during the afternoon and evening hours of the 18th. A cluster of thunderstorms developed across Sweet Grass and Stillwater Counties during the early afternoon hours as an upper level impulse approached the area. As the line moved east into a more favorable buoyancy/shear environment, (MLCAPE combined with 500 to 1500J/KG along with effective shears of 55 to 60kts) the thunderstorms quickly intensified, especially over the Billings area. ||Three large feeder-lines went down leaving 2,600 customers in Laurel and the West End of Billings without power for about an hour. In addition, the large hail, some of which was the size of walnuts and a few tennis ball size, resulted in considerable damage to homes and cars across the immediate Billings area. ||As for area crops, the hail mowed a 3,000 acre patch across sugar beet farms from Billings to Hardin. Winter wheat, 64 percent of which had been rated good to excellent, took a beating, as did malt barley. Sugar beets seemed to get the worst of the damage. The heaviest damage was found north of Hardin, west of Billings and north of Worden/Shepherd.

A upper level low centered over west-central Washington State at 6 am MDT on July 14 slowly moved eastward across western Montana by 6 am on July 15 and then moved east to central Montana later that evening. Due to the above normal amounts of atmospheric moisture with origins from the sub-tropics, the thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall in a short amount of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Glacier National Park RAWS reported 0.76 of rainfall in one hour. Other heavy rainfall reports include 0.78 40 min. ending 4:10 pm MDT 3 miles east of Creston and 0.65 in one hour via Hungry Horse RAWS at 6:11 pm MDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper level trough originating from the Gulf of Alaska moved slowly from northwest Washington on the morning of June 16 to southeast Idaho by early June 18 and then moved to the Alberta and Saskatchewan border by June 19. Several spokes of energy rotated counter-clockwise around this trough ingesting subtropical moisture originating from the Gulf of Mexico and brought periods of heavy precipitation to western Montana, totaling from 1 to up to 6 inches along the Continental Divide. The snow levels fell as low as 3,000 feet with the intense precipitation while high elevation locations in Glacier National Park received a couple feet of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person from the public located 3.4 miles southeast of Marion near Rogers Lake and at 4,063 ft. reported that just over 3 of snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong upper level low that originated from the Gulf of Alaska, moved across northwest Washington on the evening of July 23 into south-central Alberta Canada by the evening of July 24. A mid-level wave tracked north-northeast across eastern Washington State on the afternoon of July 23 helping to initiate several severe thunderstorms. These storms brought wind damage that extended into northwest Montana. A few discrete strong thunderstorms developing over southwest Montana producing large hail near Butte.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The forest service located in Rexford reported that multiple trees had fallen down or snapped off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass combined with effective winds mixing down from the mid-layers of the atmosphere to produced dangerously cold wind chills across portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of -40 or worse occurring at least every hour began as early as 5:23 PM on February 28th at the Comertown Turn-Off MT-5 DOT site and continued through morning hours of March 1st.  After a break during the day, these dangerous wind chills returned on the evening of March 1st and continued through the early morning hours of March 2nd, ending at 8:05 AM.  The coldest wind chill of -53 occurred many times during the early morning hours on March 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rather moist, energetic and cold upper level trough, originating from the Gulf of Alaska, brought strong gusty winds and snow to western Montana. Snow levels rose ahead of the cold front allowing for rain to fall across the valleys of northwest Montana. As the rain fell early on Jan. 3, cold ground temperatures allowed for slick sidewalks and secondary roads. Snow levels crashed behind the front and periods of heavy snow were observed from the Flathead Valley to Marias Pass. As the upper level trough moved through by the afternoon of Jan. 3, heavy snow bands formed and brought slick roads and low visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer in Olney reported 4 of snow with 0.39 of precipitation. Freezing rain was reported in the Libby area during the morning of Jan. 3, causing slick and slushy roads. Behind the cold front, precipitation changed to moderate and heavy snow for a period between Whitefish and Olney during the afternoon on Jan. 3. Other snow amounts: 6.0 Hand Creek SNOTEL (20 mi. SW of Olney at 5,035 ft.).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold arctic air mass and favorable atmospheric conditions allowed strong and gusty winds in excess of 60 mph to reach the surface across most of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds of 30 to 40 mph were common across most of Dawson County with a few gusts above 50 mph.  A sudden peak wind gust of 70 mph was recorded at the Lindsay Divide MT-200 DOT site at 6:07 AM on the 26th.  Sustained winds diminished below 40 mph by 8:40 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold arctic air mass and favorable atmospheric conditions allowed strong and gusty winds in excess of 60 mph to reach the surface across most of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Big Sheep Mountain RAWS site, both sustained winds of at least 40 mph and a wind gust of 61 mph were recorded at 9:08 AM on the 26th.  A peak wind gust of 63 mph was recorded at 10:08 AM.  Sustained winds diminished below 40 mph by 11:30 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration winter storm impacted most of North-Central and Southwest Montana with widespread snow and blowing snow, near record cold temperatures and |frigid wind chills. Numerous Winter Storm Warnings and Winter Weather Advisories were in effect through the event. Pacific moisture increased across Montana while a Canadian cold front from Saskatchewan/Alberta moved into the region. Precipitation continued to increase with the cold frontal passage as moist, westerly flow aloft overran arctic surface air. This pattern persisted through the weekend, bringing widespread accumulating snowfall to Central and Southwest Montana. In the system's wake, bitterly cold temperatures and wind chills moved in to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds measuring 69KT (79MPH) were reported 6 mi NE of Whitlash, MT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rather moist, energetic and cold upper level trough, originating from the Gulf of Alaska, brought strong gusty winds and snow to western Montana. Snow levels rose ahead of the cold front allowing for rain to fall across the valleys of northwest Montana. As the rain fell early on Jan. 3, cold ground temperatures allowed for slick sidewalks and secondary roads. Snow levels crashed behind the front and periods of heavy snow were observed from the Flathead Valley to Marias Pass. As the upper level trough moved through by the afternoon of Jan. 3, heavy snow bands formed and brought slick roads and low visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Shed 4 weather sensor located 6.4 miles southwest of Marias Pass near US-2 at 4,520 ft., reported heavy snow and 1-2.6 per hour snowfall rates during the afternoon on Jan. 3. It measured a storm total of 9.0 with 0.51 snow water equivalent. Other totals include: 16 at the summit(6,817 ft.) of Whitefish Ski Resort, 9 of snow in Essex, 7.8 of snow (1.18 precipitation) West Glacier COOP and 5 snow (0.46 precipitation) Whitefish COOP.
