EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass combined with effective winds mixing down from the mid-layers of the atmosphere to produced dangerously cold wind chills across portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of -40 or worse were marginal and sporadic across most of this zone, but they did persist for more than one hour at the mesonet site east of Fort Peck Dam from 6:45 AM to 8:00 AM on March 1st.  The coldest wind chill of -43 was isolated and recorded at 3:50 AM on the 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass combined with effective winds mixing down from the mid-layers of the atmosphere to produced dangerously cold wind chills across portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Steady wind chills of -40 or worse at the Navajo MT-5 DOT site began at 8:36 PM on February 28th, continued through the morning hours of March 1st, and after a break during the day on March 1st, resumed again that evening and finally ended at 7:00 AM on March 2nd.  The coldest wind chill of -53 occurred a few times in the 6:00 AM hour on March 1st.

The snow continued at MacDonald through much of the day on Jan. 4 and the MT DOT required towing units to use chains. Snow amounts include: 5.5 Discovery Ski Resort and 4 Georgetown Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong upper level jet combined with an influx of above normal Pacific moisture provided the right ingredients for heavy snowfall across the mountains of north-central Idaho and western Montana. For the 6th time during the 2013/2014 season, an arctic air mass penetrated west of the Continental Divide and brought snow, blowing snow and low visibilities to travelers later on Jan. 29 into 30. With the arctic air mass in place, several disturbances brought continued snow showers, some of which became more organized into moderate to heavy snow bands. As a side note, the 5th arctic intrusion that occurred on Jan. 26, was only a glancing blow with low impacts to southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain was reported around 12:25 pm MST on Jan. 29 by a trained spotter located 9.1 miles north-northwest of Noxon at 2,400 ft. They had reported 2.5 of snow that morning before the freezing rain began. The rain changed to all snow by the morning of Jan. 30 as colder arctic air pushed south and west through the area. Total storm snowfall amounts include: 11 Blacktail Mountain SNOTEL, 9.6 30 miles south-southeast of Libby COOP, 6 Olney COOP, and 4.8 one mile northeast of Libby COOP.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong upper level jet combined with an influx of above normal Pacific moisture provided the right ingredients for heavy snowfall across the mountains of north-central Idaho and western Montana. For the 6th time during the 2013/2014 season, an arctic air mass penetrated west of the Continental Divide and brought snow, blowing snow and low visibilities to travelers later on Jan. 29 into 30. With the arctic air mass in place, several disturbances brought continued snow showers, some of which became more organized into moderate to heavy snow bands. As a side note, the 5th arctic intrusion that occurred on Jan. 26, was only a glancing blow with low impacts to southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person from the public located 1 mile northwest of West Glacier reported 12 inches of snow that fell in 12 hours ending at 6:15 pm Jan. 29. They also reported whiteout conditions at times. An arctic cold front pushed west through Marias Pass between 7 and 8 pm MST Jan. 29 and then through Bad Rock Canyon by 11:30 pm MST. Between 6 and 12 inches of snow fell before the influx of much colder air, and this caused blowing and drifting of snow along portions of US-2 through the early morning hours of Jan. 30. Snowfall totals include: 9 Hungry Horse Dam COOP, 9 Essex, 6 Pike Creek SNOTEL located 1.6 miles SE of Marias Pass, and 5.6 West Glacier COOP.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several reinforcing bouts of arctic air bring snow and extreme cold to western Montana. By the morning of Feb. 6, many valley locations saw air temperature readings between 30 and 40 degree below zero which caused businesses and school systems to close.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer located 30 miles south-southeast of Libby at 3,440 ft., recorded a low temperature of -40 deg. F on the morning of Feb. 6. This breaks the all-time coldest temperature for the month of February for this station since continuous records began in June 1949. The previous record was -39 set back on Feb. 2, 1996. Other low temperatures include: -34 Olney and -29 Yaak 9 NNE. During the first surge of arctic air on Jan. 3, 2 to 4 inches of snow fell along US-2 from Libby to Kila causing slick roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unsettled flow brought scattered showers and cool temperatures to the region for  several days. There was a short break precipitation for most areas but another round of rain/snow, with isolated strong winds, redeveloped as a cold front moved south through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow, measuring 6.0 inches in 6 hours, was reported 1 mi WNW of Marysville, MT at the Great Divide ski area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong upper level jet combined with an influx of above normal Pacific moisture provided the right ingredients for heavy snowfall across the mountains of north-central Idaho and western Montana. For the 6th time during the 2013/2014 season, an arctic air mass penetrated west of the Continental Divide and brought snow, blowing snow and low visibilities to travelers later on Jan. 29 into 30. With the arctic air mass in place, several disturbances brought continued snow showers, some of which became more organized into moderate to heavy snow bands. As a side note, the 5th arctic intrusion that occurred on Jan. 26, was only a glancing blow with low impacts to southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Steady snow from the Pacific moisture influx continued through the afternoon of Jan. 29. A few places reported heavy snow including a weather spotter located 4 miles south-southeast of of Creston. This spotter measured 11 of snow through 8 pm MST on Jan. 29. Also a person from the public reported heavy snow in Whitefish between 5 to 8 pm an. 29. Once the arctic cold front made its way into the Flathead Valley around 11 pm MST, strong gusty winds to 36 mph caused blowing and drifting snow especially near Bad Rock Canyon. The MT DOT reported blowing and drifting snow on US-93 in the Mission Valley between 1 and 5 am MST Jan. 30 where 4 to 6 of transportable snow had fallen earlier. Total snowfall amounts include: 10 Bigfork, 10.5 13 miles south of Bigfork, 8.5 Kalispell, 8 Polson & Creston, 7 Somers, 5 Ronan and 4.3 at Kalispell International Airport. There were 11 automobile slide-offs and 16 crashes with several injuries reported between Jan. 29-30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong upper level jet combined with an influx of above normal Pacific moisture provided the right ingredients for heavy snowfall across the mountains of north-central Idaho and western Montana. For the 6th time during the 2013/2014 season, an arctic air mass penetrated west of the Continental Divide and brought snow, blowing snow and low visibilities to travelers later on Jan. 29 into 30. With the arctic air mass in place, several disturbances brought continued snow showers, some of which became more organized into moderate to heavy snow bands. As a side note, the 5th arctic intrusion that occurred on Jan. 26, was only a glancing blow with low impacts to southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person from the public reported whiteout conditions due to blowing snow near Hellgate Canyon with estimated winds gusting near 40 mph around 6:23 am MST on Jan. 30. They also reported 6 snow drifts. The MT DOT reported blowing and drifting snow on I-90 between Frenchtown to US-93 interchange at 6:17 am MST. Heavy snow bands affected portions of the Bitterroot and Missoula Valleys between Jan. 30 to 31. A multiple vehicle accident occurred on I-90 between the Orange St. and Van Buren St. exits in Missoula around 10:47 am MST on Jan. 31. Moderate to heavy snow with slick roads were reported at the time of the accident. Three day total snowfall include: 7.5 South Hills of Missoula, 5.6 Missoula International Airport, and 1 Hamilton COOP.

According to the Saddle Mountain SNOTEL located 1 mile west of Lost Trail Pass on US-93, 16 of total snow with 1.90 snow water equivalent fell between Jan. 29-30. The Lolo Pass SNOTEL recorded 20 of total snowfall with 2.30 of snow water equivalent between Jan. 28-30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong upper level jet combined with an influx of above normal Pacific moisture provided the right ingredients for heavy snowfall across the mountains of north-central Idaho and western Montana. For the 6th time during the 2013/2014 season, an arctic air mass penetrated west of the Continental Divide and brought snow, blowing snow and low visibilities to travelers later on Jan. 29 into 30. With the arctic air mass in place, several disturbances brought continued snow showers, some of which became more organized into moderate to heavy snow bands. As a side note, the 5th arctic intrusion that occurred on Jan. 26, was only a glancing blow with low impacts to southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The MT DOT sensor reported moderate to heavy snow that fell with the arctic cold front just before midnight Jan. 30. Chains were required for towing units for both MacDonald and Homestake Passes during much of the morning on Jan. 30. MT DOT indicated blowing and drifting had occurred along I-90 from Drummond to Phosphate Junction and also at MacDonald Pass. Snowfall amounts included 8 6 miles southeast Philipsburg, 6.5 Elliston,  6 Drummond, 5 Anaconda and 3.5 3 miles west of Deer Lodge.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 7 to 10 inches were reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very bitterly cold arctic air mass swung southward from the Canadian prairie, stalled over northeast Montana, and combined with some breezy to gusty winds which allowed wind chill values to plummet to very dangerous levels for the entire region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With the Beaver Hill I-94 DOT site very near the edge of Wibaux County, it was allowed to verify this warning for this zone. Consistent wind chills as cold as -40 began as early as 4:58 AM on the 5th and continued as late as 8:26 AM on the 6th. The coldest wind chill of -54 was recorded at 12:51 PM on the 5th at the Beaver Hill I-94 DOT site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the area allowed for valley cold pools to develop and temperature inversions to strengthen. This aided the development of freezing fog that caused roadways and other surfaces to become icy across portions of western Montana. A fatality occurred near Bigfork because of both operator error and black ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The MT DOT reported fog in the Kalispell area with temperatures near 20 degrees F. They reported that roads became icy and continued to have ice or black ice through the early afternoon on Jan. 19. A 42 year old male died and another 22 year old male was taken to the hospital for minor injuries after their pickup hit black ice on secondary highway 209, went out of control and ended up in the Swan River. According to the Montana Highway Patrol, the fatality was a combination of driver error and weather: going to fast, not wearing a seat belt and slipping on black ice. The Kalispell International Airport reported dense freezing fog with visibility reduced to one half mile or less beginning at 11:55 pm MST Jan. 18 through 4 am MST on Jan 19. It also reported dense freezing fog from 7:55 am through 11:55 am MST on Jan. 19. The Flathead County dispatch reported that the Kalispell City streets were a sheet of ice before daybreak on Jan 19, but said the impacts were not as great due to it being a Sunday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several reinforcing bouts of arctic air bring snow and extreme cold to western Montana. By the morning of Feb. 6, many valley locations saw air temperature readings between 30 and 40 degree below zero which caused businesses and school systems to close.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lookout Pass Ski Resort shut down due to the extreme cold between Feb. 4-5. During this period the Mullan Pass ASOS recorded wind chill values as low as -42 deg. F. On the morning of Feb. 6, a COOP observer in Alberton recorded a low of -22 deg. F while the COOP located one mile west of Haugan recorded -28 deg. F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong northwesterly mid-level jet with speeds nearing 81 mph tracked across southwest Montana and triggered mountain wave activity which allowed for some of this wind to make it down to the surface north of Avon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The MT DOT sensor located 8.1 miles north-northwest of Avon on MT-141 reported multiple wind gusts over 60 mph. When the peak wind of 75 mph occurred at 8:11 am MST, the sustained wind was 54 mph from the west. The peak wind gust at the Bert Mooney Airport was 61 mph at 10:22 am MST from the west or 270 degrees. Other wind gusts include: 51 mph Anaconda at 10:26 am, 50 mph 2 miles southwest Newcomb at 10:23 am and 48 mph 2 miles south-southeast of Warm Springs at 7:34 am MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just before midnight on January 11, precipitation developed in the higher elevations along the border of Idaho and Montana as milder and relatively moist air pushed into the region ahead of a strong Pacific storm system. An upper level trough with an above normal moisture content from subtropical origins combined with a strong upper level jet tracked through the Northern Rockies from January 11-12. This system brought heavy precipitation in the form of mountain snow & blowing snow, valley mixed precipitation including freezing rain due to below freezing ground temperatures across northwest Montana, high winds, wind loading of snow on eastern aspects in higher elevations elevating the avalanche risk and the risk for rock slides. Snow bands brought more snow to mountain passes and valley locations alike as the trough exited the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After the cold front passed through, air temperatures remained warm in the 30's and 40's and this aided the development of rain & snow showers. After sunset, weather spotters from Libby southward reported freezing rain that formed a glaze of ice on untreated surfaces. Snow covered US-2 from Happy's Inn to Marion, and there were reports of periods of heavy snow and wind gusts to 40 mph associated the showers. A weather spotter reported a foot of standing water on the Champion Haul Road near US-2 in Libby at 9:38 pm MST on Jan. 11. Total snowfall amounts include: 8 Blacktail Ski Resort, 6 at MacGregor Pass Rd. or 10 miles WSW of Marion, 2 5 miles SW of Marion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air, combined with a strong disturbance within a Northwest flow aloft, resulted in |heavy snow across the Billings Forecast Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several intense snowbands oriented north/south resulted in heavy snowfall of 7 to 14 inches across the area. Several accidents occurred on I-90 between Billings and Laurel. One accident on I-90 in Laurel resulted in a fatality.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold arctic air mass and favorable atmospheric conditions allowed strong and gusty winds in excess of 60 mph to reach the surface across most of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 60 mph wind gust was recorded at the Lufborough Hill MT-200 DOT site at 7:36 AM on the 26th.  Sustained wind diminished below 40 mph by 10:30 AM.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 58 mph was recorded at the mesonet site east of Fort Peck Dam at 8:50 AM on the 26th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold air mass from Canada combined with gusty winds to produce dangerously-cold wind chills across portions of northeast Montana, mainly for Garfield County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of at least -40 across Garfield County began as early as 4:15 AM on the 6th and lasted as late as 10:55 AM on the 6th.  The coldest wind chill of -44 was recorded at the Jordan Airport ASOS site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The seasons first severe weather outbreak occurred during the afternoon and evening hours of the 18th. A cluster of thunderstorms developed across Sweet Grass and Stillwater Counties during the early afternoon hours as an upper level impulse approached the area. As the line moved east into a more favorable buoyancy/shear environment, (MLCAPE combined with 500 to 1500J/KG along with effective shears of 55 to 60kts) the thunderstorms quickly intensified, especially over the Billings area. ||Three large feeder-lines went down leaving 2,600 customers in Laurel and the West End of Billings without power for about an hour. In addition, the large hail, some of which was the size of walnuts and a few tennis ball size, resulted in considerable damage to homes and cars across the immediate Billings area. ||As for area crops, the hail mowed a 3,000 acre patch across sugar beet farms from Billings to Hardin. Winter wheat, 64 percent of which had been rated good to excellent, took a beating, as did malt barley. Sugar beets seemed to get the worst of the damage. The heaviest damage was found north of Hardin, west of Billings and north of Worden/Shepherd.

A strong trough of low pressure, a cold front, and plentiful surface moisture combined with a moderate to very unstable atmosphere to produce and sustain many severe thunderstorms across most of eastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public estimated 80 mph winds which blew shingles off a roof and broke a window.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance combined with just enough surface moisture in an unstable atmosphere to set off a few strong to severe thunderstorms across portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported quarter-sized to golf ball-sized hail which persisted for 10 to 15 minutes, followed by 10 minutes of pea-sized hail, and a total of 2 inches of rain in about 45 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very wet and slow-moving low pressure system stalled over Montana and directed near-continuous rainfall over central and northeastern portions of the state for several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record rainfall in late August caused sustained high water and flooding at Beaver Creek near Hinsdale (BCHM8).  It rose again above its flood stage of 14 feet at 6:12 AM MST on September 3rd, crested at 14.67 feet at 6:43 PM MST on September 4th, and fell back down below flood stage at 10:30 PM MST on September 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread and at times moderate to heavy rainfall affected North-Central Montana as a broad, potent low pressure system with plenty of moisture and energy moved east over the Northern Rockies. Periods of rain and isolated thunderstorms also affected the region. Total rainfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 2 inches over North-Central Montana with only 0.25 to 0.75 inches across Southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain, measuring 2.32 inches storm total, was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid/upper level trough and associated 40-50kt wind max moved across an increasingly unstable airmass across eastern Montana. Daytime heating and large scale ascent combined with a low-level east/southeast flow helped support storm updraft rotation which produced a few severe thunderstorms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported to be rushing down Main Street. Numerous secondary streets, as well as an underpass were flooded. In addition, areas of Miles City Community College were under water.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported half dollar-sized hail.  Duration of 10 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A generally unsettled pattern persisted through this period. Lingering low level moisture, daytime heating, and weak disturbances moving through the moist westerly flow aloft, brought scattered showers with isolated thunderstorms to portions of South-Central and Southwest Montana. Hail and gusty winds were reported from some storms, and a few cells became severe. Showers and storms generally decreased each night as conditions stabilized.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail measuring 1.00 inches was reported in Great Falls, MT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms producing very large hail and damaging winds moved across portions of Southeast Montana.
A surface frontal boundary and plenty of surface moisture combined with a very unstable atmosphere to produce one brief tornado and multiple severe thunderstorms across portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative observer reported a tornado lasting less than 10 minutes and located northeast of the Hell Creek Recreation Area with no structures in the area. Radar data and lack of lower-level clouds suggest it could have been a landspout.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms producing very large hail and damaging winds moved across portions of Southeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The report was from a Big Sky Skywarn Facebook post.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread and at times moderate to heavy rainfall affected North-Central Montana as a broad, potent low pressure system with plenty of moisture and energy moved east over the Northern Rockies. Periods of rain and isolated thunderstorms also affected the region. Total rainfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 2 inches over North-Central Montana with only 0.25 to 0.75 inches across Southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain, measuring 1.88 inches in 12 hours, was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively strong trough off the Pacific Northwest coast brought southwest flow and instability to the region, resulting in scattered showers and isolated thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail, mostly pea sized with a few of 1.00 inches (quarter sized), was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance moving along the international border combined with a moderately unstable atmosphere to produce a severe thunderstorm over Sheridan county in the far northeast corner of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported mainly quarter-sized hail with some larger hail stones and winds gusting up to 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough of low pressure, a cold front, and plentiful surface moisture combined with a moderate to very unstable atmosphere to produce and sustain many severe thunderstorms across most of eastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported quarter-sized hail covering the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough of low pressure, a cold front, and plentiful surface moisture combined with a moderate to very unstable atmosphere to produce and sustain many severe thunderstorms across most of eastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported short-lived tornado moving northeast.  Path length and width have been estimated.  No damage was found.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and moderately unstable air mass ahead of a convectively-induced cyclonic circulation was in place across Eastern Montana.  Dewpoints in the middle to upper 50s with MLCAPES around 1500J/kg combined with sufficient vertical wind shear resulted in severe thunderstorms across the eastern portions of the Billings Forecast area. These thunderstorms produced large hail and a couple of brief tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado was reported by an NWS employee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The seasons first severe weather outbreak occurred during the afternoon and evening hours of the 18th. A cluster of thunderstorms developed across Sweet Grass and Stillwater Counties during the early afternoon hours as an upper level impulse approached the area. As the line moved east into a more favorable buoyancy/shear environment, (MLCAPE combined with 500 to 1500J/KG along with effective shears of 55 to 60kts) the thunderstorms quickly intensified, especially over the Billings area. ||Three large feeder-lines went down leaving 2,600 customers in Laurel and the West End of Billings without power for about an hour. In addition, the large hail, some of which was the size of walnuts and a few tennis ball size, resulted in considerable damage to homes and cars across the immediate Billings area. ||As for area crops, the hail mowed a 3,000 acre patch across sugar beet farms from Billings to Hardin. Winter wheat, 64 percent of which had been rated good to excellent, took a beating, as did malt barley. Sugar beets seemed to get the worst of the damage. The heaviest damage was found north of Hardin, west of Billings and north of Worden/Shepherd.

A strong upper level disturbance moved across Southeast Montana during the late afternoon and early evening hours of the 22nd. Strong vertical shear along with moderate buoyancy resulted in severe thunderstorms,  a few of which were supercells, across portions of the Billings Forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous reports of trees down on houses, power lines and across roads in the town of Columbus.

The tail end of the jets tream brushed over North-Central MT, coinciding with a weak cold front, to produce some showers and a few strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind gusts, measuring 52KT (60MPH), were reported.
